title: Analog Electronics
description: Analog electronics from scratch (beginner rework in progress): 15 sub-topics from DC networks through noise, layout and instrumentation. Beginner-first readings, diagrams, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: Charge, Current & Voltage: The Water Analogy
lessons.content:
Have you ever wondered what is actually moving inside a wire when you turn on a flashlight? Electricity can feel mysterious, but there is a simple picture that makes it click: **water flowing through pipes**. We will use that picture for the rest of this topic.

Everything starts with **electric charge**. Charge is a basic property of the tiny particles inside atoms; the ones that move in wires are **electrons**. Think of charge as the *water itself* — the stuff that flows. We measure an amount of charge in **coulombs (C)**. One coulomb is just a huge number of electrons bundled together.

When charge moves, we call that flow **current**. Current is the *flow rate* — how much charge passes a point each second, just like "litres per second" for water. We measure current in **amperes (A)**, usually shortened to "amps." If one coulomb of charge flows past a point every second, that is one amp ($1\\,\\text{A} = 1\\,\\text{C/s}$). A phone charger pushes a couple of amps; a flashlight bulb, much less.

But water does not flow on its own — something has to push it. In a pipe, a pump creates **pressure** that drives the water. In a circuit, that push is **voltage**. Voltage is the electrical "pressure" that drives current around the circuit, and we measure it in **volts (V)**. A single AA battery provides 1.5 V; a wall outlet (in many countries) is around 120 or 230 V. No voltage, no push, no current.

So the whole picture is: a battery acts like a pump providing **voltage** (pressure), which pushes **charge** (the water) around the loop, and the rate of that flow is **current**. Keep this water picture in your head — voltage pushes, current flows.

items.prompt: In the water analogy used here, what does electric current correspond to?
choices: The flow rate of the water
The pump that drives the water
items.explanation: Current is the flow rate of charge — how much charge passes per second — just like litres-per-second of water. Pressure corresponds to voltage, and the pump corresponds to the battery.

items.prompt: What is the unit of electric current?
items.explanation: Current is measured in amperes (amps). The volt measures voltage, the coulomb measures an amount of charge, and the watt measures power.

items.prompt: Voltage is best described as which of the following?
choices: The electrical 'pressure' that pushes charge through a circuit
The amount of charge stored in a wire
The speed of a single electron
items.explanation: Voltage is the push, the electrical pressure, that drives current around a circuit — like water pressure from a pump. Without voltage there is nothing to make charge flow.

items.prompt: If 2 coulombs of charge pass a point in a wire every second, what is the current?
items.explanation: Current is charge per second. 2 coulombs per second equals 2 amperes, because $1\\,\\text{A} = 1\\,\\text{C/s}$. Volts and coulombs are the wrong units for current.

items.prompt: A single AA battery is labeled 1.5 V. What does that number tell you?
choices: How many amps it can deliver
How much charge it holds in total
How fast electrons move inside it
items.explanation: The 1.5 V rating is the voltage — the push, or electrical pressure, the battery provides. It does not directly tell you the current, the total charge capacity, or electron speed.

items.prompt: If a circuit has voltage available but the loop is broken (a switch is off), what happens to the current?
choices: No current flows, because the path for charge is broken
Current flows faster to make up for the gap
Current flows only in the battery
Voltage and current both become zero immediately everywhere
items.explanation: Current needs a complete path (a closed loop) to flow, like water needs an unbroken pipe. With the loop broken, no current flows even though the battery still provides voltage.

items.prompt: Which everyday device delivers the most current to charge a phone quickly?
choices: A charger rated for more amps
A charger rated for fewer amps
A charger with a longer cable
A charger that is physically larger
items.explanation: Current is the flow rate of charge, so a charger that can supply more amps moves charge into the phone faster. Cable length and physical size are not what set the current.

items.prompt: What is a coulomb a measure of?
items.explanation: A coulomb is a quantity of charge — the 'water' itself. Flow rate is measured in amps, pressure in volts, and power in watts.

items.prompt: In the water analogy, which real component plays the role of the pump?
items.explanation: The battery provides the push (voltage), just as a pump provides water pressure. The wire is the pipe, and a resistor is a narrowing of the pipe (covered next).

lessons.title: Resistance, Ohm's Law & Power
lessons.content:
If voltage is the push and current is the flow, what decides *how much* current you actually get? The answer is **resistance** — and it is the third piece of the water picture.

Imagine squeezing a garden hose. The narrow spot makes it harder for water to get through, so less flows. **Resistance** is exactly that: how strongly a part of a circuit fights the flow of current. A thin or narrow path has high resistance; a wide, easy path has low resistance. We measure resistance in **ohms**, written with the Greek letter omega ($\\Omega$). A component made to have a specific resistance is called a **resistor**.

These three ideas — voltage, current, resistance — are tied together by the single most useful rule in electronics, **Ohm's law**:

$$V = I \\times R$$

In words: voltage equals current times resistance. It says that for a given resistance, more voltage (more push) gives more current (more flow); and for a given voltage, more resistance gives less current. You can rearrange it to find any one value from the other two: $I = V / R$ and $R = V / I$. A handy memory aid is the **Ohm's law triangle**: cover the quantity you want, and the triangle shows the formula.

A quick example: connect a 6 V battery across a 3 $\\Omega$ resistor. The current is $I = V/R = 6/3 = 2\\,\\text{A}$.

Moving charge through resistance also does work — it releases energy, usually as heat or light. The rate of energy use is **power**, measured in **watts (W)**. The simplest power formula is:

$$P = V \\times I$$

Power equals voltage times current. That same 6 V resistor carrying 2 A uses $P = 6 \\times 2 = 12\\,\\text{W}$. This is why a bright bulb (more watts) glows hotter than a dim one. So: resistance sets the flow, Ohm's law ties V, I, and R together, and power tells you how fast energy is being used.

items.prompt: In the water analogy, what does electrical resistance correspond to?
choices: A narrow or pinched section of pipe
items.explanation: Resistance is like a narrowing of the pipe that fights the flow. The pump is voltage, pressure is voltage, and the water is charge.

items.prompt: What is the unit of resistance, and which symbol represents it?
items.explanation: Resistance is measured in ohms, written with the Greek letter omega ($\\Omega$). Volts measure voltage, amps measure current, and watts measure power.

items.prompt: Which equation correctly states Ohm's law?
items.explanation: Ohm's law is $V = I \\times R$: voltage equals current times resistance. The other forms are not Ohm's law; adding or wrongly dividing the quantities gives nonsense units.

items.prompt: A 12 V battery is connected across a 4 $\\Omega$ resistor. What current flows?
items.explanation: Rearrange Ohm's law: $I = V / R = 12 / 4 = 3\\,\\text{A}$. Multiplying (48) or adding (16) the numbers, or dividing the wrong way (0.33), all give wrong answers.

items.prompt: A current of 2 A flows through a resistor, and 10 V appears across it. What is its resistance?
items.explanation: Rearrange Ohm's law: $R = V / I = 10 / 2 = 5\\,\\Omega$. Multiplying gives 20 and adding gives 12, both incorrect.

items.prompt: For a fixed voltage, what happens to the current if you increase the resistance?
choices: The current stays exactly the same
items.explanation: From $I = V / R$, a larger R (for the same V) gives a smaller I. More resistance fights the flow more, like pinching the hose tighter.

items.prompt: Which formula gives electrical power directly from voltage and current?
items.explanation: Power is voltage times current, $P = V \\times I$, measured in watts. It tells you how fast energy is being used. Dividing, adding, or subtracting V and I is incorrect.

items.prompt: A device runs on 5 V and draws 2 A. How much power does it use?
items.explanation: $P = V \\times I = 5 \\times 2 = 10\\,\\text{W}$. Dividing (2.5) or adding/subtracting (7 or 3) gives the wrong result.

items.prompt: A flashlight bulb glows brighter and hotter than a dim indicator LED. In terms of power, this means the bulb is:
choices: Using more volts but the same power
items.explanation: Brighter and hotter means more energy used per second, which is more power (more watts). Power, not voltage alone, sets how much light and heat is produced.

items.prompt: Using the Ohm's law triangle, how do you find the formula for current I?
choices: Cover I, and the triangle shows V over R
Cover I, and the triangle shows V times R
Cover V, and the triangle shows I over R
Cover R, and the triangle shows V times I
items.explanation: Covering I leaves V on top and R on the bottom, so $I = V / R$. Covering V would give $V = I \\times R$; the triangle is just a memory aid for the same Ohm's law.

lessons.title: Series & Parallel Circuits
lessons.content:
Real circuits have more than one resistor. The good news is that you only need two basic patterns to understand most of them: **series** and **parallel**. Think of old holiday string lights, or the way rooms in a house are wired.

In a **series** circuit, components are connected end to end, in a single line, so there is only **one path** for the current. The same current flows through every part, one after another — like water through one long pipe with several narrow spots in a row. Because each resistor adds more opposition, **series resistances simply add up**:

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \\cdots$$

So two 10 $\\Omega$ resistors in series behave like one 20 $\\Omega$ resistor. The downside: if one part breaks the chain (like one dead bulb in old series string lights), the whole circuit stops.

In a **parallel** circuit, components are connected side by side, across the same two points, giving the current **more than one path**. It is like a wide river splitting into several channels — the water divides among them. Each parallel path makes it *easier* overall for current to flow, so the combined (equivalent) resistance is actually **smaller than the smallest single resistor**. For just two resistors there is a quick formula:

$$R_{eq} = \\frac{R_1 \\times R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

For example, a 6 $\\Omega$ and a 3 $\\Omega$ resistor in parallel give $\\frac{6 \\times 3}{6 + 3} = \\frac{18}{9} = 2\\,\\Omega$ — smaller than 3. Household outlets are wired in parallel, which is why unplugging one lamp does not switch off the others.

The takeaway: series means one path and resistances add; parallel means multiple paths and the total resistance drops below the smallest one.

items.prompt: What defines a series circuit?
choices: Components are connected end to end, giving the current only one path
Components are connected side by side, giving multiple paths
Components are not connected at all
Components are connected only to the battery
items.explanation: In series, components form a single chain with one path for current, so the same current flows through each in turn. Multiple side-by-side paths describe a parallel circuit.

items.prompt: Two resistors, 10 $\\Omega$ and 15 $\\Omega$, are connected in series. What is the equivalent resistance?
items.explanation: Series resistances add: $R_{eq} = 10 + 15 = 25\\,\\Omega$. The product-over-sum (6) and similar results apply to parallel, not series.

items.prompt: In a series circuit, how does the current compare through each component?
choices: The same current flows through every component
Each component gets a different current
Current only flows through the first component
Current splits evenly among the components
items.explanation: Series gives a single path, so the same current must flow through every component in turn. Splitting of current happens in parallel circuits.

items.prompt: What is the equivalent resistance of a 6 $\\Omega$ and a 3 $\\Omega$ resistor in parallel?
items.explanation: Product over sum: $\\frac{6 \\times 3}{6 + 3} = \\frac{18}{9} = 2\\,\\Omega$. Note 2 is smaller than the smallest resistor (3), as parallel always gives. Adding them (9) would be the series answer.

items.prompt: Compared with the individual resistors, the equivalent resistance of resistors in parallel is always:
choices: Smaller than the smallest single resistor
Larger than the largest single resistor
Exactly the average of the resistors
items.explanation: Each extra parallel path makes it easier for current to flow, so the combined resistance drops below even the smallest single resistor. Summing applies to series, not parallel.

items.prompt: Why are household outlets wired in parallel rather than in series?
choices: So each device gets the full supply voltage and one failing device does not switch off the others
So less current is used overall
So all devices must be on at the same time
So the wiring uses fewer wires
items.explanation: In parallel, each outlet sees the full voltage and works independently; unplugging or a failure in one does not break the path for the others. In series, one break would stop everything.

items.prompt: Old-style holiday string lights wired in series go completely dark when a single bulb burns out. Why?
choices: A burnt bulb breaks the single shared path, so no current can flow
The other bulbs use up all the voltage
The current speeds up and overloads the rest
Parallel paths take over and short the circuit
items.explanation: A series circuit has only one path. One broken bulb opens that path, so current stops everywhere. Parallel wiring would let the others keep working.

items.prompt: Two identical 100 $\\Omega$ resistors are placed in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance?
items.explanation: $\\frac{100 \\times 100}{100 + 100} = \\frac{10000}{200} = 50\\,\\Omega$. Two equal resistors in parallel give exactly half. 200 would be the series result.

items.prompt: A 4 $\\Omega$ and a 4 $\\Omega$ resistor are connected in series, then that pair is connected to a 12 V battery. What current flows?
items.explanation: Series total is $4 + 4 = 8\\,\\Omega$. Then Ohm's law: $I = V / R = 12 / 8 = 1.5\\,\\text{A}$. Using only one resistor (3 A) ignores that they add in series.

lessons.title: Voltage Dividers, Current Dividers & Kirchhoff's Laws
lessons.content:
Now we combine what you know into two of the most useful tools in circuits, plus the two simple bookkeeping rules that make *any* circuit solvable.

Start with the **voltage divider**, probably the most common little circuit in all of electronics. Put two resistors in series across a voltage, and the voltage splits between them in proportion to their sizes. The voltage across the second resistor is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \\times \\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

If $R_1 = R_2$, the voltage splits evenly in half. This is how a volume knob taps off part of a signal. There is a catch called **loading**: the formula assumes nothing much is connected to the output. If you attach a device that draws real current, it sits in parallel with $R_2$, lowers the effective bottom resistance, and pulls $V_{out}$ down below what the formula predicts. The same effect explains why a cheap voltmeter can slightly disturb the very voltage it is trying to read — a real meter is not perfect.

Its mirror image is the **current divider**: when current reaches a parallel junction, it splits between the branches, with *more* current taking the easier (lower-resistance) path.

Finally, two rules from a scientist named Kirchhoff let you handle any circuit. **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** says that at any junction (a "node"), all the current flowing in must equal all the current flowing out — charge cannot pile up or vanish. **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** says that if you add up all the voltage rises and drops around any complete loop, they cancel to zero — you end up back where you started, at the same pressure. These two laws are the foundation under every advanced technique you will meet later. The takeaway: dividers split voltage and current predictably, and Kirchhoff's laws are simple conservation rules that keep the bookkeeping honest.

items.prompt: A voltage divider has $R_1 = R_2$. What fraction of the input voltage appears across $R_2$?
items.explanation: With equal resistors, $V_{out} = V_{in} \\times \\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{in} \\times \\frac{1}{2}$. The voltage splits evenly, so exactly half appears across $R_2$.

items.prompt: In a divider with $V_{in} = 10\\,\\text{V}$, $R_1 = 1\\,\\text{k}\\Omega$, and $R_2 = 4\\,\\text{k}\\Omega$, what is $V_{out}$ across $R_2$?
items.explanation: $V_{out} = 10 \\times \\frac{4}{1 + 4} = 10 \\times \\frac{4}{5} = 8\\,\\text{V}$. The larger resistor takes the larger share of the voltage. (2 V is the voltage across $R_1$.)

items.prompt: What is the 'loading effect' on a voltage divider?
choices: A device connected to the output draws current, lowering $V_{out}$ below the ideal formula
The divider produces more voltage than expected
The resistors heat up and increase their resistance to infinity
The input voltage rises when a load is attached
items.explanation: A real load sits in parallel with $R_2$, reducing the effective bottom resistance and pulling $V_{out}$ down. The simple formula only holds when the load draws negligible current.

items.prompt: When current reaches a junction of two parallel resistors, which branch carries more current?
choices: The branch with the lower resistance (the easier path)
The branch with the higher resistance
They always carry exactly equal current
Neither; current stops at the junction
items.explanation: Current favors the easier path, so more of it flows through the lower-resistance branch. This is the current-divider idea, the mirror image of the voltage divider.

items.prompt: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) states that at any node:
choices: The total current flowing in equals the total current flowing out
The largest current wins and the rest stop
items.explanation: KCL is conservation of charge: charge cannot pile up at a junction, so current in must equal current out. It says nothing about voltage being zero.

items.prompt: At a node, 5 A flows in and 3 A flows out on one wire. How much current flows out on the only other wire?
items.explanation: By KCL, current in equals current out: $5 = 3 + I$, so $I = 2\\,\\text{A}$. The currents leaving must add up to the 5 A that entered.

items.prompt: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) states that around any closed loop:
choices: The voltage rises and drops add up to zero
items.explanation: KVL is conservation of energy: going around a complete loop returns you to the same point at the same potential, so all the rises and drops cancel to zero.

items.prompt: A loop has a 9 V battery and two resistors in series. One resistor drops 6 V. By KVL, what voltage is dropped across the other resistor?
items.explanation: KVL: the drops around the loop must equal the 9 V rise. $9 - 6 = 3\\,\\text{V}$ is left for the second resistor. The drops sum to the supplied voltage.

items.prompt: Why can a cheap voltmeter slightly change the voltage it is measuring at a high-resistance point?
choices: The meter draws a little current, acting like a load that disturbs the circuit
The meter adds voltage of its own
The meter has no effect under any circumstance
The meter increases the battery's voltage
items.explanation: A real meter is not perfect; it draws a small current, loading the node like any other load and pulling the reading down a bit. This is the same loading effect seen in voltage dividers.

items.prompt: Which pair of rules can, together with Ohm's law, solve any basic resistor network?
choices: Kirchhoff's Current Law and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law
The voltage divider and the current divider only
items.explanation: KCL and KVL are the general conservation rules behind all circuit analysis; combined with Ohm's law they handle any resistor network. Dividers are handy shortcuts, but they are special cases.

lessons.title: Simplifying Circuits: Thevenin, Norton & Max Power
lessons.content:
Some circuits look intimidating — a tangle of resistors and batteries. The ideas here are about a wonderful simplification: no matter how complicated a circuit gets, the part you care about can often be replaced by something tiny and simple. Treat this lesson as a gentle, big-picture introduction; the goal is the concept, not heavy math.

The headline idea is **Thevenin's theorem**. It says that *any* network of sources and resistors, viewed from two output terminals, behaves exactly like a **single battery in series with a single resistor**. We call that battery $V_{th}$ (the Thevenin voltage) and that resistor $R_{th}$ (the Thevenin resistance). To the outside world, the messy box and the simple box are indistinguishable. $V_{th}$ is just the voltage measured across the terminals with nothing connected (the "open-circuit" voltage), and $R_{th}$ is how much resistance the circuit seems to have when you look back into those terminals. Real batteries already follow this idea: a battery acts like an ideal source plus a small **internal resistance**, which is why its voltage sags a little when you draw a lot of current.

**Norton's theorem** is the same idea wearing different clothes: instead of a battery in series with a resistor, you use a **current source in parallel with a resistor**. You can always convert one form into the other; this swap is called a **source transformation**. Two other classic tools, **superposition** (handle one source at a time, then add the results) and the systematic methods called **nodal** and **mesh analysis**, are just organized ways to find these values in bigger circuits.

Finally, a neat practical result, **maximum power transfer**: a source delivers the most possible power to a load when the load's resistance is **equal to** $R_{th}$. Match them and the power handed off peaks. The takeaway: any linear circuit collapses to a simple source plus a resistor, and matching the load to that resistance squeezes out the most power.

items.prompt: Thevenin's theorem says any network of sources and resistors, seen from two terminals, can be replaced by:
choices: A single voltage source in series with a single resistor
A current source with no resistor
items.explanation: The Thevenin equivalent is one voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with one resistor $R_{th}$. From the outside, it behaves exactly like the original, no matter how complex.

items.prompt: What is the Thevenin voltage $V_{th}$?
choices: The voltage across the two terminals with nothing connected to them
The voltage when the terminals are shorted together
The total current the circuit can supply
items.explanation: $V_{th}$ is the open-circuit voltage — measured at the terminals with no load attached. Shorting the terminals instead gives a current, not $V_{th}$.

items.prompt: Why does a real battery's voltage sag a little when you draw a large current from it?
choices: It has internal resistance, which drops some voltage inside the battery
It runs out of charge instantly
Its voltage is always exactly fixed no matter what
items.explanation: A real battery is an ideal source plus a small internal resistance (its own $R_{th}$). Drawing current causes a voltage drop across that internal resistance, so the terminal voltage sags.

items.prompt: How does a Norton equivalent differ from a Thevenin equivalent?
choices: Norton uses a current source in parallel with a resistor instead of a voltage source in series
Norton uses two resistors and no source
Norton has no resistor at all
They are identical in form and cannot be told apart
items.explanation: Norton swaps the voltage-source-in-series picture for a current-source-in-parallel picture. They describe the same behavior and can be converted into each other.

items.prompt: Converting a Thevenin equivalent into a Norton equivalent (or vice versa) is called:
items.explanation: Swapping between the two equivalent forms is a source transformation. Superposition and Kirchhoff's laws are different tools used to analyze circuits.

items.prompt: What is the basic idea of superposition in a circuit with several sources?
choices: Find the effect of each source acting alone, then add the results together
Use only the biggest source and ignore the rest
Multiply all the source values together
Remove every source and solve what is left
items.explanation: Superposition handles one source at a time (turning the others off) and adds up the separate results. It works because these circuits respond proportionally to each source.

items.prompt: Nodal analysis and mesh analysis are best described as:
choices: Systematic, organized methods for solving larger circuits using Kirchhoff's laws
Methods that only work on a single resistor
items.explanation: Nodal and mesh analysis are organized bookkeeping methods built on KCL and KVL for tackling bigger circuits in a structured way.

items.prompt: The maximum power transfer idea says a source delivers the most power to a load when:
choices: The load resistance equals the source's Thevenin resistance $R_{th}$
The load resistance is exactly double $R_{th}$
items.explanation: Power handed to the load peaks when the load is matched to the source resistance, $R_{load} = R_{th}$. A zero or infinite load delivers little or no power.

items.prompt: A source has a Thevenin resistance of 8 $\\Omega$. For maximum power transfer, what load resistance should you connect?
items.explanation: Maximum power transfer occurs when the load matches the source resistance, so $R_{load} = R_{th} = 8\\,\\Omega$. Other values deliver less power to the load.

items.prompt: Why is replacing a complicated circuit with its Thevenin equivalent useful?
choices: It lets you predict how the circuit behaves with any load using just one source and one resistor
It permanently destroys the original circuit
It removes the need for any resistors
items.explanation: Once you know $V_{th}$ and $R_{th}$, you can quickly work out what happens for any load without re-solving the whole tangle. That simplicity is the whole point of the theorem.

lessons.title: Meet the Capacitor
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Ever notice a camera flash needs a second to "charge up" before it can fire? That pause is a *capacitor* filling with energy.

**Build.** A capacitor is two metal plates with an insulator between them. Push voltage across it and it stores electric charge on those plates. Think of it as a tiny rechargeable bucket: the bigger the bucket, the more charge it holds for a given push. The size of the bucket is its **capacitance** (symbol C), measured in **farads (F)**. The rule is simple: charge = capacitance times voltage, written Q = C x V. A capacitor cannot change its voltage instantly — it fills and empties along a smooth curve (see the diagram). Once it is fully charged, no more current flows, so a capacitor **blocks steady (DC) current**. But if the voltage keeps changing (an AC signal), the capacitor keeps charging and discharging, so it **lets changing signals through**.

**Recap.** A capacitor stores charge in an electric field, fills/empties gradually, blocks steady DC, and passes changing signals.

items.prompt: What does a capacitor mainly store?
choices: Electric charge in an electric field
items.explanation: A capacitor holds charge on two plates, storing energy in the electric field between them. Magnetic-field storage is an inductor's job.

items.prompt: What is the unit of capacitance?
items.explanation: Capacitance is measured in farads (F). Ohms measure resistance, henries measure inductance, volts measure voltage.

items.prompt: Using Q = C x V, if you double the voltage across a fixed capacitor, the stored charge:
items.explanation: Charge is proportional to voltage for a fixed C, so doubling V doubles Q.

items.prompt: Once an ideal capacitor is fully charged and the voltage is steady (DC), the current through it is:
items.explanation: A full capacitor opposes any further change; with steady voltage no more charge flows, so current is zero. This is why capacitors block DC.

items.prompt: Which statement about a capacitor is true?
choices: It has no effect on AC signals
items.explanation: Charging/discharging takes time (the smooth curve), so a capacitor's voltage changes gradually, never instantaneously.

items.prompt: How does a capacitor treat a constantly changing (AC) signal?
choices: It lets it pass (keeps charging and discharging)
It turns it into steady DC
It stores it and never releases it
items.explanation: A changing voltage keeps the capacitor charging and discharging, so changing signals pass through while steady DC is blocked.

items.prompt: A larger capacitance (bigger 'bucket') means the capacitor:
choices: Stores more charge for the same voltage
items.explanation: Q = C x V: a bigger C holds more charge at the same voltage.

items.prompt: In a camera flash, the capacitor's role is best described as:
choices: Slowly storing energy, then releasing it in a quick burst
items.explanation: It charges up gradually from the battery, then dumps that stored energy rapidly into the flash tube — store slowly, release fast.

lessons.title: Meet the Inductor
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A heavy flywheel is hard to get spinning and, once spinning, hard to stop. An *inductor* does the same thing with electric current.

**Build.** An inductor is just a coil of wire. When current flows through it, it stores energy in a **magnetic field** around the coil. Its key habit: it **resists changes in current**. Try to increase the current and it pushes back; try to cut the current off and it tries to keep it going (which is why switching off a coil can cause a spark). The size of this effect is its **inductance** (symbol L), measured in **henries (H)**. Because steady (DC) current isn't changing, a fully-settled inductor lets DC pass easily (a coil of wire is nearly a plain wire to DC). But a fast-changing (AC) signal is exactly what it fights. So an inductor is the mirror image of a capacitor: it **passes DC and opposes fast changes**.

**Recap.** An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field and resists changes in current — passing DC while fighting fast AC.

items.prompt: Where does an inductor store its energy?
choices: In an electric field between plates
In a magnetic field around the coil
items.explanation: Current through the coil creates a magnetic field that holds the energy — the opposite of a capacitor's electric field.

items.prompt: What is the unit of inductance?
items.explanation: Inductance is measured in henries (H). Farads are for capacitance.

items.prompt: An inductor's main behavior is that it:
items.explanation: An inductor opposes changes in current (capacitors oppose changes in voltage — they are mirror images).

items.prompt: How does an ideal inductor treat steady DC current?
items.explanation: Steady DC isn't changing, so the inductor doesn't fight it — a coil looks almost like a plain wire to DC.

items.prompt: Why can a spark jump when you suddenly switch off a coil (like a relay)?
choices: The inductor tries to keep current flowing
items.explanation: An inductor resists the sudden drop in current and briefly produces a large voltage to keep it going — enough to arc across the opening switch.

items.prompt: The flywheel analogy fits an inductor because a flywheel:
choices: Resists changes in its speed of spinning
items.explanation: A flywheel resists changes in rotation just as an inductor resists changes in current; both store energy in motion/field.

items.prompt: Compared with a capacitor, an inductor:
choices: Is the mirror image: passes DC, fights fast AC
items.explanation: Capacitor: blocks DC, passes AC. Inductor: passes DC, opposes fast AC. They are opposites.

items.prompt: Adding more turns to the coil generally gives an inductor:
items.explanation: More turns build a stronger magnetic field for the same current, increasing inductance.

lessons.title: Impedance: Resistance That Depends on Frequency
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why does a cheap speaker sound tinny, letting through high notes but not deep bass? Because capacitors and inductors treat different frequencies differently.

**Build.** **Impedance** (symbol Z, measured in ohms) is like resistance, but it can change with **frequency** — how fast the signal wiggles per second. For a capacitor, the impedance is Z_C = 1/(2 x pi x f x C). Notice f is on the bottom: as frequency **rises**, a capacitor's impedance **falls**, so high-frequency signals pass more easily. For an inductor it's the opposite: Z_L = 2 x pi x f x L, so as frequency rises, its impedance **rises**, blocking fast signals more. (A plain resistor, by contrast, has the same resistance at every frequency.) This frequency-dependent behavior is exactly what lets engineers build **filters** that keep some frequencies and reject others.

**Recap.** Impedance is frequency-dependent resistance: a capacitor's impedance drops as frequency rises; an inductor's rises. That difference is the basis of filtering.

items.prompt: What is impedance?
choices: Opposition to current that can depend on frequency
items.explanation: Impedance (Z, in ohms) generalizes resistance to include the frequency-dependent effects of capacitors and inductors.

items.prompt: As frequency increases, a capacitor's impedance:
items.explanation: Z_C = 1/(2*pi*f*C): frequency is in the denominator, so higher f means lower impedance — high frequencies pass more easily.

items.prompt: As frequency increases, an inductor's impedance:
items.explanation: Z_L = 2*pi*f*L: frequency is on top, so higher f means higher impedance — inductors fight fast signals more.

items.prompt: At 0 Hz (steady DC), an ideal capacitor's impedance is:
items.explanation: Putting f = 0 into 1/(2*pi*f*C) gives infinite impedance — another way of saying the capacitor blocks DC.

items.prompt: At 0 Hz (steady DC), an ideal inductor's impedance is:
items.explanation: Z_L = 2*pi*f*L with f = 0 gives zero — the inductor passes DC like a plain wire.

items.prompt: How does an ideal resistor's resistance change with frequency?
items.explanation: An ideal resistor has the same resistance at all frequencies; only capacitors and inductors are frequency-dependent.

items.prompt: A capacitor placed in the signal path tends to pass high frequencies and block low ones because it:
choices: Has low impedance at high frequency
Has low impedance at low frequency
items.explanation: Its impedance is low at high frequency and high at low frequency, so it lets highs through and blocks lows — a high-pass behavior.

items.prompt: Doubling the frequency through a capacitor (everything else fixed) changes its impedance to:
items.explanation: Z_C is inversely proportional to f, so doubling f halves the impedance.

lessons.title: Real Components: Parasitics & the j-Operator
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A real capacitor is never a *perfect* capacitor — and that surprises people when a circuit misbehaves at high frequency.

**Build.** Every real component has small unwanted extras called **parasitics**. A real capacitor has a little resistance (called ESR) and a little inductance (ESL) from its leads; a real inductor has resistance in its wire. Because a capacitor carries hidden inductance, at a high enough frequency it stops acting like a capacitor and starts acting like an inductor — the point where they balance is its **self-resonant frequency** (the dip in the diagram). Engineers also use a bookkeeping symbol, **j**, to mark that a capacitor's or inductor's effect is "90 degrees out of step" with a resistor's: it stores and returns energy rather than burning it as heat. Finally, a **transformer** is two coupled coils — a changing current in one induces a voltage in the other, letting you step voltage up or down by the ratio of turns.

**Recap.** Real parts have parasitics (ESR/ESL) and a self-resonant frequency; the symbol j marks energy-storing (90-degree) behavior; transformers couple two coils to change voltage.

items.prompt: What are 'parasitics' in a real component?
choices: Small unwanted electrical properties, like stray resistance or inductance
items.explanation: Parasitics are unintended extras — e.g. a capacitor's lead inductance (ESL) and resistance (ESR) — that matter most at high frequency.

items.prompt: Above its self-resonant frequency, a real capacitor starts behaving like:
items.explanation: Its hidden lead inductance takes over above self-resonance, so it acts inductive rather than capacitive.

items.prompt: A capacitor's 'ESR' refers to its small unwanted:
items.explanation: ESR = Equivalent Series Resistance, the tiny resistance in a real capacitor that wastes a little energy as heat.

items.prompt: The symbol j is used to mark that a capacitor or inductor:
choices: Burns energy as heat like a resistor
Stores and returns energy, 90 degrees out of step with a resistor
items.explanation: j is bookkeeping for the 90-degree phase behavior of energy-storing elements, distinguishing them from heat-dissipating resistors.

items.prompt: Unlike a resistor, an ideal capacitor or inductor:
choices: Stores energy and gives it back
items.explanation: Resistors dissipate energy as heat; capacitors and inductors store it (in electric or magnetic fields) and return it to the circuit.

items.prompt: A transformer works by:
choices: Using a changing current in one coil to induce a voltage in a nearby coil
items.explanation: Two magnetically-coupled coils: a changing current in the primary induces a voltage in the secondary.

items.prompt: A transformer with more turns on the output coil than the input coil will:
items.explanation: Output voltage scales with the turns ratio, so more output turns gives a higher (stepped-up) voltage.

items.prompt: Why do transformers generally NOT work with steady DC?
choices: DC current isn't changing, so it can't induce a voltage in the other coil
items.explanation: Induction needs a CHANGING magnetic field; steady DC makes a steady field that induces nothing in the secondary.

lessons.title: Sinusoids and the Phasor Representation
lessons.content:
## Hook

The electricity in your wall socket does not sit still. It pushes one way, eases off, then pushes the other way, over and over, dozens of times a second. A sound wave does the same; so does the signal in a radio. Anything that **wiggles up and down over time** in this smooth, repeating way is called **AC** (alternating current). The opposite is **DC** (direct current), which holds steady like a battery. This lesson is about describing those wiggles.

## Build

The most basic wiggle is the **sine wave** (also called a *sinusoid*) — the smooth curve you get by tracking the height of a point on a spinning wheel, or the path of a child on a swing. Just three numbers describe it completely:

- **Amplitude** — how big the swing is (how far up and down it goes from the middle). For voltage this is the peak voltage, written $V_m$.
- **Frequency** — how *fast* it wiggles: how many full up-and-down cycles happen each second. The unit is the **hertz (Hz)**, meaning cycles per second. UK/EU mains is 50 Hz; US mains is 60 Hz. The time for one full cycle is the **period** $T$, and frequency is just $f = 1/T$.
- **Phase** — the *timing* of the wiggle: where in its cycle it starts. Two waves can be identical in size and speed but one starts a little earlier than the other. That head-start (or lag) is the phase, measured as an angle.

Written as a formula, a voltage sine wave is $v(t) = V_m\cos(\omega t + \phi)$. Here $V_m$ is amplitude, $\phi$ (the Greek letter *phi*) is phase, and $\omega$ (*omega*) is the **angular frequency**, just the ordinary frequency rescaled into radians per second: $\omega = 2\pi f$. The $t$ is time.

Now the key idea. In a circuit driven by one steady AC source, *every* voltage and current ends up wiggling at the **same frequency** — only their size and timing differ. So carrying the wiggling $\omega t$ part through every line of algebra is wasted effort: it is the same for everyone. A **phasor** throws it away and keeps only what changes: the **size and timing**, drawn as an arrow (size = length, timing = the angle it points). Working with fixed arrows is far easier than juggling moving curves — it turns calculus into simple arrow arithmetic.

## Recap

AC is a smooth repeating wiggle described by amplitude (how big), frequency (how fast), and phase (its timing). A phasor is a shorthand arrow that captures just the size and timing, so we can do algebra instead of wrestling with curves.

items.prompt: What is the everyday difference between AC and DC?
choices: AC wiggles up and down over time; DC holds a steady value
AC is always larger than DC
DC wiggles up and down; AC is steady
They are two names for the same thing
items.explanation: AC (alternating current) repeatedly reverses and changes, like the mains supply or a sound wave. DC (direct current) stays steady, like a battery. The 'wiggle vs. steady' picture is the core distinction.

items.prompt: Which three quantities completely describe a sine wave?
choices: Amplitude (how big), frequency (how fast), and phase (its timing)
items.explanation: A sinusoid is pinned down by how big the swing is (amplitude), how many cycles per second it makes (frequency), and where in its cycle it starts (phase). Resistance and current are circuit responses, not descriptions of the wave's shape.

items.prompt: What does the 'amplitude' of a voltage sine wave tell you?
choices: How many cycles happen each second
How far up and down the voltage swings from the middle (the peak size)
Where in its cycle the wave starts
How long one full cycle lasts
items.explanation: Amplitude is the size of the swing — the peak voltage $V_m$. The number of cycles per second is frequency, where it starts is phase, and the time for one cycle is the period.

items.prompt: Frequency is measured in hertz (Hz). What does one hertz mean?
choices: One full cycle (one complete up-and-down) per second
items.explanation: A hertz is one cycle per second. So 60 Hz mains completes 60 full wiggles every second. Volts measure size, not speed; radians measure angle.

items.prompt: A wave completes one full cycle every 0.02 seconds. What is its frequency?
items.explanation: Frequency is one divided by the period: $f = 1/T = 1/0.02 = 50$ Hz. The period and frequency are reciprocals — a shorter period means a faster (higher-frequency) wave.

items.prompt: Two sine waves have the same amplitude and frequency, but one reaches its peak slightly before the other. What is different between them?
items.explanation: Same size and same speed, but a difference in timing, is exactly what phase describes. One wave is shifted earlier or later in its cycle relative to the other.

items.prompt: In the everyday sense used here, what is a phasor?
choices: An arrow that captures just a wave's size and timing, leaving out the constant wiggling part
Another word for the period of a wave
A type of AC power supply
items.explanation: A phasor is a shorthand arrow: its length is the wave's size (amplitude) and the direction it points is the wave's timing (phase). The fast wiggling part is the same for every signal, so we drop it.

items.prompt: Why is it safe to leave the wiggling part out when we use phasors for a circuit driven at one frequency?
choices: Because the wiggling part is the same for every voltage and current, so it cancels out
Because the wiggling part is too small to matter
Because phasors change the frequency of the circuit
Because the circuit has no current flowing
items.explanation: When one steady AC source drives a circuit, every signal wiggles at the same frequency. That shared part contributes equally everywhere, so it can be set aside, leaving only the size and timing that actually differ.

items.prompt: What is the main practical benefit of using phasors (arrows) instead of the full wiggling curves?
choices: It makes the circuit run faster
It lets you do simple algebra with arrows instead of harder math on moving curves
It removes the need for a power source
items.explanation: The payoff is purely in the math: fixed arrows can be added and scaled with ordinary algebra, which is far easier than tracking curves that move in time. The physical circuit is unchanged.

items.prompt: In the formula $v(t)=V_m\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, what does the symbol $\phi$ stand for?
items.explanation: $\phi$ (phi) is the phase — the timing offset. $V_m$ is the amplitude, $\omega$ sets how fast it wiggles, and $t$ is time. Naming each symbol keeps the formula from looking mysterious.

lessons.title: RMS Values, Power Factor, and the Power Triangle
lessons.content:
## Hook

Your wall outlet is labelled "120 V" (or "230 V"), yet the voltage is a sine wave that is constantly changing — it is even zero twice every cycle. So what single number does that label mean? It is the **RMS** value, and it answers a very practical question: *how much steady DC voltage would do the same job?*

## Build

**RMS** stands for **root-mean-square**, which is just the recipe for computing it (square the wave, average it, take the square root). But you do not need the recipe to understand it. The plain-English meaning is:

> The RMS value of an AC wave is the **steady DC value that would heat a resistor by the same amount**.

So "120 V RMS" mains delivers the same heating power to a heater as a steady 120 V battery would. For a sine wave, the RMS value is the peak divided by about 1.41:

$$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_m. $$

That is why "120 V" mains actually peaks at about 170 V. We use RMS because it lets us compute real power with the same simple formulas we used for DC.

**Power in AC circuits.** With DC, power is just voltage times current. With AC there is a twist: voltage and current can be out of step (out of phase) by some angle $\phi$. That timing mismatch splits the power into three kinds:

- **Real power** $P$ — the power that does actual work and produces heat or motion. $P = V_{rms} I_{rms}\cos\phi$, measured in **watts (W)**. The factor $\cos\phi$ is called the **power factor**.
- **Reactive power** $Q$ — energy that sloshes back and forth into capacitors and inductors each cycle but does no net work. $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms}\sin\phi$, measured in **VAR** (volt-amperes reactive).
- **Apparent power** $S$ — the total the wires and source must actually carry. $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$, measured in **VA** (volt-amperes).

These three fit together as a right triangle — the **power triangle** — with $P$ along the bottom, $Q$ standing up, and $S$ as the slanted side: $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$, and the power factor is $\cos\phi = P/S$.

A power factor of 1 (voltage and current perfectly in step) means all the power does work. A power factor near 0 means the wires carry a lot of current that accomplishes nothing useful — which wastes capacity, so utilities discourage it.

## Recap

RMS is the steady-DC equivalent of an AC wave ($V_m/\sqrt2$ for a sine). Real power (watts) does work; reactive power (VAR) just sloshes; apparent power (VA) is the total carried. The power factor $\cos\phi$ is the fraction that does useful work.

items.prompt: In plain language, what does the RMS value of an AC wave tell you?
choices: The highest voltage the wave ever reaches
The steady DC value that would heat a resistor by the same amount
The average of the peak and zero
items.explanation: RMS is defined by equal heating: it is the DC value that delivers the same average power to a resistor. The highest value is the peak (a different number), and frequency is unrelated.

items.prompt: A sine wave has a peak voltage of 170 V. What is its RMS value?
items.explanation: For a sine wave, $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt2 \approx 0.707 \times 170 \approx 120$ V. This is why ordinary US mains, with a 170 V peak, is called '120 V'. Half the peak (85 V) is a common wrong guess.

items.prompt: Why do we bother using RMS values for AC instead of the peak value?
choices: RMS lets us calculate real power with the same simple formulas we use for DC
RMS is always a rounder number
Peaks change with frequency but RMS does not
items.explanation: RMS is built around equal heating, so power formulas look just like the DC ones (power equals voltage times current, suitably). That convenience is the whole point.

items.prompt: Which kind of power does actual work — producing heat, light, or motion?
items.explanation: Real power, measured in watts, is the part that does useful work. Reactive power just sloshes in and out of capacitors and inductors; apparent power is the total the wires carry.

items.prompt: What is reactive power (measured in VAR)?
choices: Power lost forever as heat in the wires
Energy that sloshes back and forth into capacitors and inductors but does no net work
The total power the source must supply
The peak power at the top of the wave
items.explanation: Reactive power is energy that gets stored and returned each cycle by reactive parts (capacitors and inductors). It does no net work, but it still uses up wire and source capacity to carry it.

items.prompt: In the power triangle, how are real power $P$, reactive power $Q$, and apparent power $S$ related?
choices: $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ (S is the hypotenuse of a right triangle)
items.explanation: They form a right triangle: $P$ along the base, $Q$ vertical, and $S$ as the slanted hypotenuse, so $S=\sqrt{P^2+Q^2}$. They do not simply add, because $P$ and $Q$ are at right angles.

items.prompt: A load draws 5 A at 120 V (both RMS) with a power factor of 0.8. What is the real power?
items.explanation: Real power $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi = 120 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 480$ W. The full $120 \times 5 = 600$ is the apparent power (VA); only 80% of it does work.

items.prompt: A power factor of 1 (voltage and current perfectly in step) means:
choices: All of the power carried does useful work
None of the power does useful work
The frequency is at its maximum
items.explanation: Power factor is $\cos\phi = P/S$. A value of 1 means $P = S$: all the apparent power becomes real, working power. A purely resistive load (like a heater) behaves this way.

items.prompt: Why do power companies dislike a low power factor?
choices: It raises the frequency of the supply
It means the wires carry a lot of current that does no useful work, wasting capacity
It lowers the voltage at every home
items.explanation: With a low power factor, the apparent power (and so the current) is large compared with the useful real power. That extra current heats conductors and uses up source and transformer capacity for nothing, so utilities discourage it.

items.prompt: A purely reactive load has its current and voltage 90 degrees out of step ($\cos 90^\circ = 0$). How much real (working) power does it consume?
items.explanation: Real power is $P = V_{rms} I_{rms}\cos\phi$, and $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, so $P = 0$. All the energy just sloshes in and out (pure reactive power); none is turned into useful work.

lessons.title: Frequency Response and the Transfer Function H(jw)
lessons.content:
## Hook

Why does a cheap speaker sound muffled, missing the crisp highs? Why does turning a tone knob make a sound brighter or darker? Circuits often treat different frequencies differently — boosting some, weakening others. The tool that captures "what this circuit does to each frequency" is the **transfer function**.

## Build

Think of a circuit as a box with a signal going in and a signal coming out. The **transfer function** answers a simple question, frequency by frequency:

> If I feed in a wiggle at this frequency, how big and how shifted is the wiggle that comes out?

We write it $H(j\omega)$ — read as "H of omega." It is the **output divided by the input**, worked out using phasors (the size-and-timing arrows from earlier). At each frequency it gives you two things:

- **Gain** — how the output size compares to the input size. A gain of 2 doubles the signal; a gain of 0.5 halves it. This is the magnitude, written $|H|$.
- **Phase shift** — how much the output is nudged earlier or later in time compared to the input.

Gain and phase plotted against frequency together make up the **frequency response** of the circuit — its complete "frequency personality."

**A familiar example: the low-pass filter.** A resistor followed by a capacitor makes a circuit that passes low frequencies through almost untouched but quietens high frequencies — exactly the muffled-speaker effect. Its transfer function is

$$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega R C}. $$

The frequency where it starts noticeably cutting things is the **corner frequency** (or cutoff), $f_c = 1/(2\pi R C)$. Below it, signals pass nearly full strength; above it, they fade out the higher you go. Right at the corner, the output is about 0.707 of the input — a level engineers call the **−3 dB point**, which we will unpack in the next lesson.

**Poles and zeros.** When you write a transfer function as a fraction, the frequencies that make the *bottom* blow up are called **poles**, and the ones that make the *top* vanish are called **zeros**. You do not need the heavy math yet — just the idea: poles tend to bend the response *downward* as frequency rises, zeros bend it *upward*. The low-pass filter above has a single pole at its corner frequency, which is why it rolls off above the corner.

## Recap

A transfer function $H(j\omega)$ tells you, at each frequency, how much a circuit changes a signal's size (gain) and timing (phase shift). Together over all frequencies that is the frequency response. Poles pull the response down; zeros push it up.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does a circuit's transfer function tell you?
choices: How much power the circuit wastes as heat
For each frequency, how big and how shifted the output wiggle is compared to the input
The total resistance of the circuit
How long the circuit takes to switch on
items.explanation: The transfer function describes, frequency by frequency, what the circuit does to a signal: how the output size compares to the input (gain) and how the timing shifts (phase). It is output divided by input.

items.prompt: The transfer function gives two pieces of information at each frequency. What are they?
choices: Gain (size change) and phase shift (timing change)
items.explanation: At each frequency it tells you the gain (how the output amplitude compares to the input) and the phase shift (how much the output is nudged earlier or later). Plotted versus frequency, these are the frequency response.

items.prompt: If a circuit has a gain of 2 at some frequency, what happens to a signal at that frequency?
items.explanation: Gain is the ratio of output size to input size. A gain of 2 means the output is twice as big as the input. The frequency itself never changes in a linear circuit — only the size and timing.

items.prompt: What does a low-pass filter do to a signal?
choices: Passes high frequencies and blocks low ones
Passes low frequencies and quietens high ones
Boosts every frequency by the same amount
items.explanation: A low-pass filter lets low frequencies through nearly untouched and weakens high frequencies — the muffled-speaker effect. A circuit that did the opposite would be a high-pass filter.

items.prompt: What is the corner (cutoff) frequency of a filter?
choices: The frequency at which the circuit stops working entirely
The frequency around which the filter starts noticeably changing the signal's size
The highest frequency a circuit can produce
The frequency where the phase shift is zero
items.explanation: The corner (or cutoff) frequency marks where the filter begins to act noticeably — for a low-pass, where the output has dropped to about 0.707 of the input. It is not a hard wall; signals fade gradually beyond it.

items.prompt: An RC low-pass filter has $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. Roughly what is its corner frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$?
items.explanation: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC) = 1/(2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.0000001) \approx 1591$ Hz, about 1.6 kHz. Getting the powers of ten right is the main trap here.

items.prompt: What is the 'frequency response' of a circuit?
choices: Its gain and phase shift plotted across all frequencies
The time it takes to respond to a switch
The amount of heat it produces
items.explanation: The frequency response is the full picture of how the circuit treats every frequency: gain and phase plotted versus frequency. It is the circuit's complete 'frequency personality'.

items.prompt: In a transfer function written as a fraction, what is a 'pole'?
choices: A frequency that makes the top of the fraction vanish
A frequency that makes the bottom of the fraction blow up, tending to bend the response downward
The highest gain a circuit can reach
A point where the phase is exactly zero
items.explanation: A pole is a value that drives the denominator (bottom) toward zero. In practice poles bend the gain downward as frequency rises. The opposite — a value that makes the top vanish — is a zero, which bends the response upward.

items.prompt: How many poles does the simple RC low-pass filter $H = 1/(1 + j\omega RC)$ have, and what does that pole do?
choices: Zero poles; it boosts all frequencies
One pole, at the corner frequency, which makes the response roll off above the corner
Two poles, which make it pass high frequencies
One zero and no poles, which keeps the response flat
items.explanation: The single factor in the denominator gives one pole, located at the corner frequency. That pole is exactly why the gain falls off (rolls off) as frequency rises above the corner.

items.prompt: Besides changing a signal's size, what else can a circuit do to it, as captured by the transfer function?
choices: Shift its timing earlier or later (a phase shift)
Increase the number of cycles per second
items.explanation: A linear circuit cannot change a signal's frequency; it can only change the size (gain) and the timing (phase shift). The phase part of the transfer function describes that timing nudge.

lessons.title: Bode Plots: Sketching Frequency Response by Hand
lessons.content:
## Hook

A filter might pass a 100 Hz tone at full strength, a 1 kHz tone at a tenth, and a 100 kHz tone at a thousandth. Plotting numbers that range from 1 down to 1/1000 on ordinary graph paper is hopeless — the small ones vanish into the axis. A **Bode plot** is a clever kind of graph that makes such huge ranges easy to read and even easy to sketch by hand.

## Build

A Bode plot shows a circuit's frequency response using two tricks that tame big numbers.

**Trick 1: a logarithmic frequency axis.** Instead of marking the bottom axis 1, 2, 3, 4..., we mark it 10, 100, 1000, 10000 — each step multiplies by ten. One such ten-times step is called a **decade**. This squeezes a vast range of frequencies into a readable width.

**Trick 2: decibels for the gain.** The vertical axis shows gain in **decibels (dB)**, a compressed scale. The conversion is $\text{gain in dB} = 20\log_{10}(\text{gain})$. You only need a few landmarks:

- A gain of 1 (no change) is **0 dB**.
- Ten times bigger is **+20 dB**; ten times smaller is **−20 dB**.
- Twice as big is about **+6 dB**; half as big is about **−6 dB**.
- The special 0.707 level from the last lesson is **−3 dB**.

Because logarithms turn multiplication into addition, the curves become nearly straight lines that you can sketch piece by piece. For the simple one-pole low-pass filter:

- **Below the corner frequency:** the gain is flat (a level line near 0 dB) — low frequencies pass freely.
- **Above the corner frequency:** the gain falls along a straight slope of **−20 dB per decade**. That means every time the frequency goes up tenfold, the gain drops by 20 dB (to one-tenth its size).
- **Right at the corner:** the true curve dips about 3 dB below where the two straight lines would meet.

The attached figure shows exactly this: a flat line, a bend at the corner frequency, then a downward −20 dB/decade slope, with the real curve sagging 3 dB at the corner. A **pole** always bends the slope down by 20 dB/decade; a **zero** does the mirror image, bending it up by 20 dB/decade. To sketch any filter, you just mark each corner frequency and tilt the line down (for a pole) or up (for a zero) at each one.

## Recap

A Bode plot uses a log frequency axis and a decibel gain axis so huge ranges fit and curves become straight lines. One pole rolls the gain off at −20 dB/decade past its corner; a zero tilts it up the same amount. The corner is the −3 dB point.

items.prompt: Why does a Bode plot use a logarithmic frequency axis (10, 100, 1000...) instead of an ordinary one?
choices: To make the circuit run at higher frequencies
To squeeze a very wide range of frequencies into a readable width
Because frequency cannot be plotted normally
items.explanation: Circuits operate across enormous frequency ranges. A log axis, where each step multiplies by ten, fits that whole range onto one readable graph. It does not change the circuit itself.

items.prompt: On a Bode frequency axis, what is a 'decade'?
choices: A factor-of-ten step in frequency (for example, 100 Hz to 1000 Hz)
items.explanation: A decade is a tenfold change in frequency, such as 100 Hz to 1 kHz. A factor-of-two step is called an octave instead. Decades are the natural unit on a log axis.

items.prompt: A gain of 1 (the signal comes out the same size it went in) is how many decibels?
items.explanation: Using $20\log_{10}(1) = 0$, a gain of 1 is 0 dB. Decibels measure a ratio, and 'no change' (ratio 1) sits at zero on the dB scale.

items.prompt: Making a signal ten times bigger corresponds to how many decibels?
items.explanation: $20\log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB. So a factor of ten is +20 dB (and a factor of one-tenth is −20 dB). A factor of two, by contrast, is about +6 dB.

items.prompt: On a Bode magnitude plot, what is the gain doing well below the corner frequency of a one-pole low-pass filter?
choices: Flat (a level line near 0 dB) — low frequencies pass freely
items.explanation: Below the corner, a low-pass filter barely affects the signal, so the gain line is flat near 0 dB. The roll-off only begins above the corner frequency.

items.prompt: Above its corner frequency, a single-pole low-pass filter's gain falls at −20 dB per decade. What does that mean?
choices: The gain drops by 20 dB every second
Every tenfold increase in frequency drops the gain by 20 dB (to one-tenth)
The gain drops by 20 dB at every frequency
The frequency drops by a factor of ten
items.explanation: Minus 20 dB per decade means: each time the frequency goes up by a factor of ten, the gain falls by 20 dB, which is a factor of ten smaller. It describes the slope of the falling line, not a change over time.

items.prompt: A one-pole low-pass rolls off at −20 dB/decade. How far below the flat passband is the gain two decades above the corner?
items.explanation: At −20 dB per decade, two decades give $2 \times 20 = 40$ dB of drop. So the gain is 40 dB below the flat passband level.

items.prompt: Right at the corner frequency, how does the true curve compare to where the two straight asymptote lines meet?
choices: It sits exactly on the meeting point
It is about 3 dB below the meeting point
It is about 20 dB above it
It is about 6 dB above it
items.explanation: The straight-line asymptotes meet at the corner, but the real curve sags about 3 dB below that point — its biggest departure from the straight-line sketch. This is the −3 dB point.

items.prompt: How does a zero's effect on a Bode magnitude plot differ from a pole's?
choices: A zero bends the slope up by 20 dB/decade, the mirror image of a pole's downward bend
A zero has no effect at all
A zero bends the slope down twice as fast as a pole
A zero changes the frequency axis to a linear one
items.explanation: A pole tilts the gain down by 20 dB/decade at its corner; a zero is the mirror image, tilting it up by 20 dB/decade. Sketching a filter is just tilting the line down at each pole and up at each zero.

items.prompt: A circuit has a flat gain up to 100 Hz, one pole at 100 Hz, and a second pole at 10 kHz. What is the slope of the gain between 100 Hz and 10 kHz?
items.explanation: Below 100 Hz the gain is flat. The first pole at 100 Hz tilts it down by 20 dB/decade, so between the two corners the slope is −20 dB/decade. Above 10 kHz the second pole adds another −20, making it −40 dB/decade.

lessons.title: First-Order RC & RL: The Time Constant
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a switch and a light comes on instantly. But charge a capacitor through a resistor and the voltage does not jump — it *rises smoothly*, fast at first, then slower, easing into its final value. Combining a resistor with a capacitor (or an inductor) gives a circuit that takes **time** to respond. That delay is the whole idea here.

**Build.** A *capacitor* stores energy as voltage; an *inductor* stores energy as current. A *resistor* limits how fast charge can flow. Put one storage element with one resistor and you have a **first-order circuit** — "first-order" just means there is one storage element, so one smooth exponential rise or fall.

The key number is the **time constant**, written $\tau$ (the Greek letter "tau"). It tells you *how fast the circuit settles*. For a resistor-and-capacitor (RC) circuit, $\tau = R \times C$. For a resistor-and-inductor (RL) circuit, $\tau = L / R$. Either way $\tau$ is measured in seconds.

Think of filling a glass under a tap that you slowly close as the glass fills: it gushes at first, then trickles. In **one** $\tau$ the voltage covers about **63%** of its full change. After about **5** $\tau$ it is within 1% of done — close enough to call "settled." That is the **5-tau rule**.

One more idea: an RC circuit *averages* a fast wiggly signal (acting like an **integrator**, smoothing it) but *passes the edges* of a slow signal (acting like a **differentiator**, reacting to changes). Which one depends on whether the signal changes faster or slower than $\tau$.

**Recap.** One resistor plus one storage element = a smooth exponential response whose speed is set by $\tau$ ($RC$ or $L/R$): 63% in one $\tau$, settled by five.

items.prompt: In the charging curve shown, why does the voltage rise quickly at first and then slow down?
choices: The resistor heats up and changes value
As the capacitor fills, less voltage is left to gain, so charging slows
The capacitor pushes back harder than the source
The time constant increases over time
items.explanation: The closer the capacitor gets to its final voltage, the smaller the remaining gap, so it charges more and more slowly — an exponential approach. The resistor and $\tau$ stay constant; only the remaining gap shrinks.

items.prompt: What does the time constant $\tau$ tell you about a first-order circuit?
choices: How fast it settles toward its final value
How much current it draws forever
items.explanation: $\tau$ is the 'how fast it settles' number. A larger $\tau$ means a slower response. It does not set the final value (the source does that), and a first-order RC/RL circuit has no resonance.

items.prompt: For a resistor-capacitor (RC) circuit, the time constant is:
items.explanation: For RC, $\tau = R \times C$ (in seconds). The $L/R$ form is for an RL circuit, and $1/(RC)$ is a frequency, not a time.

items.prompt: About what fraction of its total change does a first-order circuit complete in one time constant?
items.explanation: In one $\tau$ the response covers roughly 63% of the way to its final value. (37% is what is left to go — a common mix-up.) It is not finished until about 5 $\tau$.

items.prompt: The '5-tau rule' says that after about five time constants, the circuit is:
choices: Within about 1% of its final value (essentially settled)
items.explanation: After ~5 $\tau$ the response is within roughly 1% of its final value, so engineers treat it as settled. A first-order circuit eases in smoothly; it does not oscillate.

items.prompt: A $10\,\mu\text{F}$ capacitor charges through a $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistor. What is the time constant?
items.explanation: $\tau = R \times C = (10\times10^{3})(10\times10^{-6}) = 0.1\,\text{s} = 100\,\text{ms}$. Watch the powers of ten: micro is $10^{-6}$ and kilo is $10^{3}$.

items.prompt: An RC circuit with $\tau = 2\,\text{ms}$ is driven from $0\,\text{V}$ toward $5\,\text{V}$. The capacitor voltage at $t = 2\,\text{ms}$ (one $\tau$) is approximately:
items.explanation: At one $\tau$ the capacitor has covered about 63% of the $5\,\text{V}$ swing: $0.63 \times 5 \approx 3.16\,\text{V}$. It does not reach $5\,\text{V}$ until about $5\tau$.

items.prompt: For a resistor-inductor (RL) circuit, if you increase $R$ (keeping $L$ fixed), the response becomes:
choices: Faster, because $\tau = L / R$ gets smaller
Slower, because $\tau = L / R$ gets larger
items.explanation: For RL, $\tau = L / R$. A bigger $R$ makes $\tau$ smaller, so the current rises and falls faster. (The $R\times C$ form belongs to RC circuits, not RL.)

items.prompt: Why can't the voltage across a capacitor jump instantly from one value to another?
choices: A sudden voltage jump would need infinite current to flow
It actually can jump instantly in real circuits
items.explanation: Current into a capacitor is proportional to how fast its voltage changes, so an instant jump would demand infinite current — impossible. That is why capacitor voltage always changes smoothly over time.

items.prompt: An RC circuit smooths a fast, wiggly signal into a steady average. In this role it is acting as an:
items.explanation: When the signal changes much faster than $\tau$, the capacitor can only respond to the average, smoothing the wiggles — that is integrator behavior. A differentiator does the opposite, reacting to sudden changes.

lessons.title: Second-Order RLC: Ringing & Damping
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Pluck a guitar string and it does not stop at once — it *rings*, the note slowly fading away. A circuit with **both** an inductor and a capacitor does the same thing: give it a sudden jolt and the voltage can swing past its target, swing back, and wobble before finally settling. How much it wobbles is the story of this lesson.

**Build.** Add a second storage element and you get a **second-order RLC circuit** (one resistor, one inductor, one capacitor). Now energy can slosh back and forth between the inductor and the capacitor, like a pendulum trading speed for height. The resistor acts like friction — it bleeds energy away and *calms the wobble*. We call that calming effect **damping**.

How strongly a circuit is damped decides one of three behaviors:

- **Underdamped** (light damping): it overshoots and **rings** — wobbles up and down — before settling. A lightly plucked string.
- **Critically damped** (just-right damping): it reaches the final value as fast as possible with **no overshoot**. The sweet spot.
- **Overdamped** (heavy damping): it crawls slowly to the final value, no wobble, but sluggish. Like a door closer that eases shut.

Engineers measure damping with a number called the **damping ratio**, written $\zeta$ ("zeta"). The rule is simple: $\zeta < 1$ rings (underdamped), $\zeta = 1$ is the no-overshoot sweet spot (critically damped), and $\zeta > 1$ is the slow, sluggish case (overdamped). More resistance in a series RLC means heavier damping, so less ringing.

**Recap.** Two storage elements let energy slosh, so a jolt makes the circuit ring; the resistor damps it. Light damping rings, just-right damping settles fastest, heavy damping crawls.

items.prompt: In the diagram, which curve overshoots its final value and wobbles before settling?
items.explanation: The underdamped response ($\zeta < 1$) shoots past the final value and rings before settling. The critically damped and overdamped curves never overshoot.

items.prompt: What makes a circuit 'second-order' rather than 'first-order'?
choices: It has two energy-storage elements (an inductor and a capacitor)
It runs at twice the frequency
items.explanation: Order counts energy-storage elements. Two of them (L and C) make it second-order, which is what allows energy to slosh back and forth and the circuit to ring. Resistors do not count toward the order.

items.prompt: In an RLC circuit, what role does the resistor play?
choices: It acts like friction, bleeding energy away and calming the wobble (damping)
It has no effect on the transient
items.explanation: The resistor is the source of damping — like friction on a pendulum, it removes energy each cycle so the ringing dies out. The inductor and capacitor store the sloshing energy.

items.prompt: A circuit that overshoots and rings before settling is:
items.explanation: Overshoot plus ringing is the signature of an underdamped response (light damping, $\zeta < 1$). Overdamped crawls in slowly; critically damped settles fast with no overshoot.

items.prompt: Which damping condition reaches the final value fastest WITHOUT overshooting?
items.explanation: Critical damping is the boundary case: it gets to the final value as quickly as possible while still not overshooting. Underdamped is faster to first reach the level but overshoots; overdamped never overshoots but is slow.

items.prompt: The damping ratio is written $\zeta$ (zeta). A circuit with $\zeta = 0.2$ is:
items.explanation: Any $\zeta < 1$ is underdamped, and $0.2$ is well below 1, so this circuit rings noticeably before settling. $\zeta = 1$ would be critical and $\zeta > 1$ overdamped.

items.prompt: In a series RLC, what happens to the ringing if you increase the resistance?
choices: The damping increases, so the ringing dies out faster (or stops)
items.explanation: More series resistance means more 'friction,' i.e. heavier damping. The wobble fades faster, and with enough R the circuit stops ringing entirely (critical or overdamped).

items.prompt: Why is a plucked guitar string a good analogy for an underdamped RLC circuit?
choices: It oscillates and slowly fades, just like ringing that decays
It only moves once and stops instantly
items.explanation: A plucked string vibrates back and forth and the sound gradually fades — exactly like the decaying oscillation (ringing) of an underdamped circuit after a jolt. Air resistance is the string's 'damping.'

items.prompt: Compared to an underdamped circuit, an overdamped circuit reaching the same final value will:
choices: Get there more slowly but with no overshoot or ringing
items.explanation: Overdamped means heavy damping: the response crawls toward the final value with no overshoot and no ringing. It does reach the final value — just sluggishly.

items.prompt: Energy in an undamped LC pair sloshes back and forth between the inductor and capacitor. This is most like:
choices: A pendulum trading speed for height
A switch turning on and off
items.explanation: Like a pendulum trading kinetic energy (speed) for potential energy (height), an LC circuit trades energy between the inductor's magnetic field and the capacitor's electric field. The resistor is what eventually stops the swing.

lessons.title: Resonance: A Circuit's Favorite Frequency
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Push a child on a swing. Shove randomly and not much happens. But time your pushes to the swing's natural rhythm and small pushes build a big arc. That special rhythm is the swing's favorite frequency — its **resonance**. Circuits with an inductor and a capacitor have a favorite frequency too.

**Build.** An inductor and a capacitor each *resist* alternating current, but in opposite ways, and the amount depends on frequency. This frequency-dependent resistance is called **reactance**. At one special frequency the inductor's reactance and the capacitor's reactance become **equal and cancel out**. That frequency is the **resonant frequency**, written $f_0$ ("f-naught"):

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

In words: the resonant frequency depends only on the inductor ($L$) and capacitor ($C$) sizes — bigger $L$ or $C$ means a lower (slower) favorite frequency. The resistor does not move $f_0$.

At resonance the two reactances cancel, so the circuit behaves in a striking way. In a **series RLC** (parts in a line), the cancellation lets current flow most easily — current peaks at $f_0$. In a **parallel RLC** ('tank' circuit), the opposite happens: the circuit blocks current from the source most strongly at $f_0$, while energy circulates round and round inside.

This is exactly the swing: drive the circuit near $f_0$ and the response builds up large, just like timed pushes building a big swing. Drive it at the wrong frequency and little happens. That is how a radio 'tunes in' one station — it resonates at that station's frequency and ignores the rest.

**Recap.** An L and a C give a circuit a favorite frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ where their reactances cancel; driving at $f_0$ builds a big response, like pushing a swing in rhythm.

items.prompt: In the swing analogy, why do well-timed small pushes build a big swing?
choices: Each push is timed to the swing's natural rhythm, so the motion adds up
items.explanation: When pushes match the swing's natural rhythm (its resonant frequency), each push adds energy in step with the motion, so it builds up. A circuit driven at its resonant frequency $f_0$ responds the same way.

items.prompt: Resonance in an RLC circuit happens at the frequency where:
choices: The inductor's and capacitor's reactances are equal and cancel
items.explanation: At resonance the inductive and capacitive reactances are equal and opposite, so they cancel. This leaves the circuit looking purely resistive at $f_0$.

items.prompt: What is 'reactance'?
choices: A component's frequency-dependent opposition to alternating current
items.explanation: Reactance is how strongly an inductor or capacitor opposes AC, and it changes with frequency. At resonance the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel each other.

items.prompt: The resonant frequency of an LC circuit is given by:
items.explanation: $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$. The $L$ and $C$ are under the square root in the denominator, so larger $L$ or $C$ lowers $f_0$. The resistor does not appear.

items.prompt: If you increase both $L$ and $C$ in a resonant circuit, the resonant frequency $f_0$:
items.explanation: Since $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$, larger $L$ or $C$ makes the denominator bigger, so $f_0$ goes down (a slower favorite frequency). To raise $f_0$ you would shrink $L$ or $C$.

items.prompt: Does changing the resistor shift the resonant frequency $f_0$?
choices: No, $f_0$ depends only on L and C
Only if R is very large
items.explanation: The formula $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ contains no R, so the resistor does not move the resonant frequency. (R does affect how sharp the resonance is, which is the next lesson.)

items.prompt: In a SERIES RLC circuit at resonance, the current is:
