items.explanation: In a series circuit the canceling reactances leave only the small resistance, so current flows most easily and peaks at $f_0$. (A parallel 'tank' does the opposite — it blocks source current at $f_0$.)

items.prompt: A tank circuit has $L = 10\,\mu\text{H}$ and $C = 100\,\text{pF}$. About what is $f_0$?
items.explanation: $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC}) = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{(10\times10^{-6})(100\times10^{-12})}) \approx 5\,\text{MHz}$. Multiply $L\times C$ first, take the square root, then divide into $1/(2\pi)$.

items.prompt: How does a radio 'tune in' one station and ignore the others?
choices: It resonates at the chosen station's frequency, responding strongly there and weakly elsewhere
It changes the speed of light
items.explanation: A tunable LC circuit is set so its resonant frequency $f_0$ matches the desired station. Like a swing pushed in rhythm, it responds strongly at that frequency and weakly at others, selecting one station.

items.prompt: Driving an RLC circuit at a frequency far from $f_0$ produces:
choices: A huge response, bigger than at $f_0$
items.explanation: Away from resonance the reactances no longer cancel, so the response is small — just like shoving a swing at the wrong rhythm. The big build-up only happens near $f_0$.

lessons.title: Quality Factor Q, Bandwidth & Selectivity
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Two swings: one with smooth, well-oiled joints keeps swinging for a long time after a push; a rusty one stops quickly. The smooth swing is *choosy* — it really only likes its own rhythm. Resonant circuits are the same, and we measure that choosiness with a number called **Q**.

**Build.** The **quality factor**, written $Q$, says how *sharp* or *choosy* a resonant circuit is. A high $Q$ means the circuit stores energy well and loses little each cycle, so it rings for a long time and responds to only a narrow band of frequencies near $f_0$. A low $Q$ loses energy fast and responds to a wide range of frequencies. The smooth swing is high-$Q$; the rusty one is low-$Q$.

The spread of frequencies a resonator responds to is its **bandwidth** (BW) — measured between the two frequencies where the response drops to about 70% of its peak (the 'half-power' points). Q and bandwidth are tied together by a simple rule:

$$\text{BW} = \frac{f_0}{Q}$$

In words: a high $Q$ gives a *narrow* bandwidth. Narrow bandwidth means the circuit is **selective** — good at picking out one frequency and rejecting neighbors, like a radio separating two nearby stations.

Here is the catch, and it ties back to ringing: high $Q$ also means light damping, so a very selective (high-$Q$) circuit *rings for a long time* when jolted. Low $Q$ settles quickly but is not choosy. Engineers trade one against the other: selectivity versus quick settling.

**Recap.** $Q$ measures choosiness; high $Q$ = narrow bandwidth = very selective, but it rings longer. The link is $\text{BW} = f_0 / Q$.

items.prompt: On the resonance plot, the two marked points on either side of the peak mark the edges of what?
choices: The bandwidth (the half-power points where response drops to ~70%)
items.explanation: The two markers are the half-power (about 70% of peak) points; the frequency span between them is the bandwidth. A narrower span between them means a higher $Q$ and a more selective circuit.

items.prompt: What does a high quality factor $Q$ tell you about a resonant circuit?
choices: It loses a lot of energy each cycle
It is sharp and choosy — narrow bandwidth, responds to a narrow range of frequencies
It has a higher resonant frequency
items.explanation: High $Q$ means little energy lost per cycle, so the resonance is sharp and selective with a narrow bandwidth. $Q$ does not set $f_0$ itself — L and C do that.

items.prompt: A 'bandwidth' of a resonator is best described as:
choices: The spread of frequencies it responds to (between the half-power points)
items.explanation: Bandwidth is the width of the frequency range where the circuit responds strongly, measured between the half-power (~70% of peak) points on each side of $f_0$.

items.prompt: The relationship between bandwidth, resonant frequency, and Q is:
items.explanation: $\text{BW} = f_0 / Q$. A higher $Q$ shrinks the bandwidth (narrower, more selective); a lower $Q$ widens it.

items.prompt: A resonator has $f_0 = 10\,\text{MHz}$ and $Q = 50$. Its bandwidth is:
items.explanation: $\text{BW} = f_0 / Q = 10\,\text{MHz} / 50 = 0.2\,\text{MHz} = 200\,\text{kHz}$.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a filter to be 'selective'?
choices: It responds to every frequency equally
It can pick out one frequency and reject nearby ones
items.explanation: Selectivity is the ability to single out one frequency while rejecting neighbors. It comes from a narrow bandwidth, which comes from a high $Q$ — like a radio separating two close stations.

items.prompt: Two filters share the same $f_0$. Filter A has $Q = 100$; filter B has $Q = 10$. Which is correct?
choices: A is broader and less selective
B is narrower and more selective
A is narrower, more selective, and rings longer
Q has no effect on bandwidth
items.explanation: Higher $Q$ (filter A) gives a narrower bandwidth ($f_0/Q$), more selectivity, and lighter damping — so it also rings longer when jolted. That is the selectivity-versus-settling tradeoff.

items.prompt: Why does a very high-Q (very selective) circuit ring for a long time after a sudden jolt?
choices: High Q means light damping, so energy fades slowly
items.explanation: High $Q$ means very little energy is lost each cycle (light damping), so a jolt rings for many cycles before fading. This is the flip side of being highly selective.

items.prompt: Using the well-oiled versus rusty swing comparison, the well-oiled swing corresponds to:
choices: High Q (keeps swinging, choosy about rhythm)
items.explanation: A well-oiled swing loses little energy, keeps going a long time, and really only likes its own rhythm — that is exactly a high-$Q$, narrow-bandwidth, selective resonator. The rusty swing is low-$Q$.

items.prompt: If you want a filter that settles quickly after a jolt rather than ringing, you should choose:
choices: A lower Q (broader bandwidth, more damping)
items.explanation: Lower $Q$ means heavier damping and a broader bandwidth, so the circuit settles quickly with little ringing — at the cost of being less selective. It is a direct tradeoff with selectivity.

lessons.title: What a Diode Is: A One-Way Valve for Current
lessons.content:
Think of a turnstile at a stadium: people can walk through one way but are blocked the other way. A **diode** is the same idea for electric current. It is a tiny electronic component that lets current flow in one direction and blocks it in the other. That is the single most important thing to remember: **a diode is a one-way valve for current.**

Why would anyone want that? Because lots of everyday gadgets need current pushed in just one direction. A diode is what stops a battery from being charged backwards, and it is the heart of the part inside a phone charger that turns wobbly AC wall power into steady one-direction DC (more on that later).

Inside, a diode is a small crystal of silicon with two halves joined at a boundary called the **PN junction** (the meeting point of two specially-treated regions of the crystal). You do not need the physics yet. Just picture the junction as the turnstile gate.

A diode has two ends. The **anode** is the side current enters when the valve is open; the **cathode** is the side it exits. On the schematic symbol, current is allowed to flow in the direction the little triangle points (toward the bar). Push current the way the triangle points and it flows; push it the other way and the diode blocks it.

**Recap:** a diode allows current one way (through the triangle) and blocks it the other way. Everything else about diodes builds on this one-way-valve picture.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what does a diode do?
choices: It stores electric charge like a battery
It lets current flow one way and blocks it the other way
It increases the voltage of a signal
It blocks current in both directions
items.explanation: A diode is a one-way valve for current: it conducts in one direction and blocks the other. It does not store charge (that's a capacitor or battery) and it does not amplify (that's a transistor or amplifier).

items.prompt: Which everyday object is the best analogy for how a diode behaves?
choices: A turnstile or one-way door that only lets you pass one direction
A spring that pushes back when squeezed
A bucket that fills up with water
A revolving door you can pass through either way
items.explanation: A turnstile or one-way door allows passage in only one direction, just like a diode passes current one way and blocks the other. A bucket (storage) is more like a capacitor; a two-way revolving door would allow both directions, which a diode does not.

items.prompt: The two terminals of a diode are called the:
items.explanation: A diode has an anode (where current enters when conducting) and a cathode (where it exits). Source/drain belong to a MOSFET, base/collector to a BJT, and plates to a capacitor.

items.prompt: On a diode's schematic symbol, current is allowed to flow:
choices: In the direction the triangle points (toward the bar)
Against the direction the triangle points
Only when the diode is cold
items.explanation: The triangle acts like an arrowhead: conventional current flows in the direction it points, from anode through to the cathode bar. The bar end blocks current trying to come back the other way.

items.prompt: The boundary inside a diode where its two treated regions meet is called the:
items.explanation: A diode is built around a PN junction, the meeting point of a p-type and an n-type region of the crystal. This junction is what gives the diode its one-way behavior. (You can picture it as the turnstile gate.)

items.prompt: Why is a diode useful in something like a phone charger?
choices: It helps turn back-and-forth AC wall power into one-direction DC
It stores energy for when the power goes out
It boosts the wall voltage to a higher value
items.explanation: Wall power is AC, meaning the current sloshes back and forth. Because a diode only passes current one way, it is the key part in converting that AC into the steady DC a phone needs. Storing energy is a capacitor/battery job; raising voltage is a transformer/converter job.

items.prompt: If you connect a diode so current is pushed against the triangle's direction, what happens?
choices: The diode stores the current for later
items.explanation: Pushing current the wrong way (against the triangle) is the blocked direction, so essentially no current flows. This is exactly the one-way-valve behavior.

items.prompt: Which statement about a diode is TRUE?
choices: It amplifies a weak signal into a strong one
It conducts equally well in both directions
It passes current in one direction and blocks the other
It always blocks current no matter how it is connected
items.explanation: The defining property of a diode is one-way conduction. It does not amplify (that needs a transistor), it is not symmetric, and it does not block in both directions (then it would be useless).

items.prompt: The diode is also important historically because it is the conceptual stepping-stone to which more powerful device?
items.explanation: A transistor is essentially built from junctions like the diode's, and learning the diode first makes transistors far easier. Resistors, transformers, and capacitors are passive parts unrelated to the PN junction idea.

items.prompt: A diode that allows current one way and blocks it the other
items.explanation: See the diagram: with the battery one way (forward) current flows through the diode; flip the battery (reverse) and the diode blocks it, like a one-way valve.

lessons.title: The 0.7-Volt Drop, Forward/Reverse Bias, and the LED
lessons.content:
Last time we said a diode is a one-way valve. Now let's add two practical facts you will use constantly.

**Fact 1: it takes a little push to open the valve.** A diode does not start conducting the instant you apply any voltage. It needs a small forward voltage first, like a door that needs a gentle shove before it swings open. For the most common diodes (made of **silicon**), that shove is about **0.7 volts** (V). Once current is flowing, the diode keeps roughly 0.7 V across itself; we call this the **forward voltage drop**. Voltage here means electrical 'pressure'. So if you put 5 V across a silicon diode and a resistor in series, the diode 'uses up' about 0.7 V and the rest appears across the resistor.

**Fact 2: bias just means which way you connect it.**
- **Forward bias** = connected so current flows the allowed way (anode more positive). The valve is open, about 0.7 V drop, current flows.
- **Reverse bias** = connected the blocking way (cathode more positive). The valve is shut; only a tiny, usually ignorable trickle leaks through.

A picture of this is the diode's **I-V curve**: a graph of current (I) versus voltage (V). It stays near zero until about 0.7 V (the **knee**), then shoots up steeply.

The **LED** (Light-Emitting Diode) is a diode that glows when forward-biased, which is why it only lights up if connected the right way around. LEDs are the little indicator lights on nearly every gadget. They need a higher forward voltage than 0.7 V (often 1.8 to 3 V depending on color) and always need a resistor in series to limit current.

**Recap:** a silicon diode needs about 0.7 V to turn on, drops about 0.7 V while conducting (forward bias), blocks when reverse-biased, and an LED is just a diode that lights up the right way around.

items.prompt: Roughly how much voltage does a typical silicon diode need before it starts to conduct?
items.explanation: A silicon diode's forward 'knee' is about 0.7 V. Below that it barely conducts; above it, current rises steeply. It does not need 5 V or 12 V to begin conducting.

items.prompt: 'Forward bias' means the diode is connected so that:
choices: Current flows in the allowed (conducting) direction
The diode glows blue regardless of type
items.explanation: Forward bias means the anode is made more positive so current flows the way the valve allows. Reverse bias is the blocked direction. Bias just describes how you connect it relative to its one-way direction.

items.prompt: When a diode is reverse-biased, how much current flows through it?
choices: Only a tiny, usually negligible trickle
items.explanation: In reverse bias the valve is shut, so only a tiny leakage current flows, which we usually ignore. That is the whole point of the one-way behavior.

items.prompt: You put 5 V across a silicon diode in series with a resistor, with the diode forward-biased. Roughly how much voltage appears across the resistor?
items.explanation: The diode drops about 0.7 V while conducting, so the rest, 5 - 0.7 = 4.3 V, appears across the resistor. The diode does not take all 5 V, and it does not leave 0 V for the resistor.

items.prompt: On a diode's I-V curve, the 'knee' refers to:
choices: The flat reverse region where no current flows
The point near 0.7 V where current starts rising steeply
The maximum voltage the diode can survive
The temperature at which the diode fails
items.explanation: The knee is the bend near 0.7 V where the curve turns from nearly flat to a steep rise; it marks where the silicon diode 'turns on'. It is not a temperature or a failure point.

items.prompt: What does the abbreviation LED stand for?
items.explanation: LED stands for Light-Emitting Diode: a diode that gives off light when forward-biased. It is still a diode, so it only conducts (and glows) one way.

items.prompt: An LED in a circuit does not light up. A likely simple cause is:
choices: LEDs only light in total darkness
It needs to be cooled first
LEDs never light on the first try
items.explanation: Because an LED is a diode, it only conducts and glows when connected the right way (forward-biased). Connected backwards, it blocks current and stays dark. Flip it around and add a series resistor.

items.prompt: Why is a resistor almost always placed in series with an LED?
choices: To make the LED change color
To store charge for the LED
To limit the current so the LED is not destroyed
items.explanation: Once an LED is past its forward voltage it would draw huge current and burn out; the series resistor limits that current to a safe value. It does not change color or store charge.

items.prompt: Compared with a plain silicon diode's ~0.7 V, an LED's forward voltage is typically:
items.explanation: LEDs need more forward voltage to light, commonly 1.8 to 3 V depending on color (blue/white need more than red). This is higher than the ~0.7 V of an ordinary silicon diode.

items.prompt: The diode I-V curve (current versus voltage)
choices: Graph of the diode I-V characteristic
items.explanation: The curve stays near zero current until about 0.7 V (the knee), then rises steeply: this is the silicon diode turning on in forward bias.

lessons.title: Rectifiers: Turning AC into DC
lessons.content:
Here's the big payoff of the one-way valve. Wall power is **AC** (alternating current): the current sloshes back and forth, reversing direction many times a second (50 or 60 times, depending on the country). But most electronics need **DC** (direct current): a steady flow in one direction. The circuit that converts AC into DC is a **rectifier**, and it is built from diodes.

**Half-wave rectifier (simplest).** One diode. It passes only the halves of the wave that push the allowed way and blocks the rest. The output is a series of bumps with gaps between them, all on one side. Wasteful, but it works.

**Full-wave rectifier (better).** A clever arrangement of diodes flips the blocked halves over instead of throwing them away, so you get a bump for every half of the wave, twice as often, with no gaps. The most popular version is the **bridge rectifier**: four diodes arranged in a diamond. No matter which way the AC pushes, two of the diodes route the current the same way to the output.

Either way, the output is still lumpy, pulsing DC, not smooth. To smooth it we add a **smoothing capacitor** (filter capacitor) across the output. A capacitor stores charge like a small reservoir: it fills up at each peak and then keeps the voltage from sagging too far during the dips. The leftover up-and-down wobble that remains is called **ripple**.

The bigger the capacitor, the smaller the ripple. A rough rule for the peak-to-peak ripple is:

$$V_{ripple} \\approx \\frac{I_{load}}{f \\times C}$$

where $I_{load}$ is the current the circuit draws, $C$ is the capacitor size, and $f$ is how often the capacitor gets refilled per second. Full-wave refills twice as often as half-wave, so for the same smoothness it needs only about half the capacitor: another reason full-wave bridges are so common.

**Recap:** a rectifier uses diodes to turn back-and-forth AC into one-direction DC; a smoothing capacitor flattens the lumps, and the leftover wobble is ripple, which shrinks with a bigger capacitor (or full-wave rectification).

items.prompt: What is the job of a rectifier?
choices: To store energy in a magnetic field
items.explanation: A rectifier converts alternating current (AC), which reverses direction, into direct current (DC), which flows one way. It is built from diodes because each diode only passes current one way. Turning DC into AC is an inverter's job.

items.prompt: What is the difference between AC and DC?
choices: AC is higher voltage; DC is always lower voltage
AC reverses direction repeatedly; DC flows steadily in one direction
AC is only used in batteries; DC only in wall outlets
items.explanation: AC (alternating current) sloshes back and forth, reversing direction many times a second; DC (direct current) flows steadily one way. Wall power is AC; batteries give DC. It is about direction, not which one is higher voltage.

items.prompt: A half-wave rectifier uses how many diodes, and what does its output look like?
choices: One diode; bumps on one side with gaps in between
Four diodes; a perfectly smooth line
Two diodes; a pure sine wave
No diodes; just the AC unchanged
items.explanation: A half-wave rectifier uses a single diode that passes only the allowed halves of the wave, giving bumps with gaps where the blocked halves used to be. It is simple but wasteful and harder to smooth.

items.prompt: A bridge rectifier is made of:
choices: Four diodes arranged in a diamond
items.explanation: A bridge rectifier uses four diodes in a diamond arrangement so that, whichever way the AC pushes, current is routed the same way to the output. This makes it a full-wave rectifier without needing a special transformer.

items.prompt: Compared with a half-wave rectifier, a full-wave rectifier output:
choices: Throws away more of the wave
Produces a bump for every half of the wave, with no gaps
Is just the AC with no change
items.explanation: A full-wave rectifier flips the blocked halves instead of discarding them, so there is a bump for every half-cycle and no gaps. That makes the output easier to smooth than half-wave.

items.prompt: What does the smoothing (filter) capacitor do in a power supply?
choices: It stores charge and holds the voltage up between peaks, smoothing the lumps
items.explanation: The capacitor acts like a small reservoir: it charges up at each peak and supplies current during the dips, keeping the voltage from sagging. This smooths the lumpy rectified output toward steady DC.

items.prompt: The small leftover up-and-down wobble on the smoothed DC output is called:
items.explanation: Ripple is the residual wobble that the smoothing capacitor cannot fully remove. A bigger capacitor reduces ripple, as does using a full-wave rectifier (which refills the capacitor more often).

items.prompt: Using the rough rule V_ripple = I_load / (f x C), how do you reduce the ripple?
choices: Use a larger capacitor (and/or refill it more often)
Draw more current from the load
items.explanation: Ripple is inversely proportional to C and to the refill frequency f, so a larger capacitor or refilling more often (full-wave) lowers ripple. Drawing more load current (larger I_load) makes ripple worse.

items.prompt: Why does a full-wave rectifier need only about half the smoothing capacitor of a half-wave rectifier for the same smoothness?
choices: Because it refills the capacitor twice as often, so the voltage sags less between refills
Because it lowers the load current
Because it raises the AC voltage
items.explanation: Full-wave produces a bump every half-cycle, so the capacitor is topped up twice as often. With less time to sag between refills, a smaller capacitor achieves the same low ripple. This is a key practical advantage of full-wave rectification.

items.prompt: After rectifying and smoothing, the output is best described as:
choices: Steady DC with a little leftover ripple
items.explanation: Rectifying makes the current one-directional and the smoothing capacitor flattens it, leaving mostly steady DC with a small ripple. It is not pure AC anymore, and it is certainly not zero.

items.prompt: AC input, then rectified bumps, then smoothed DC
choices: Waveforms: AC vs rectified vs smoothed DC
items.explanation: The three panels show the journey: a back-and-forth AC wave, then all-positive bumps after full-wave rectifying, then nearly flat DC with small ripple after the smoothing capacitor.

items.prompt: A full-wave bridge rectifier with a smoothing capacitor
choices: Circuit: bridge rectifier feeding a capacitor and load
items.explanation: The four-diode diamond steers the AC so the output is always one direction; the capacitor across the load smooths it into DC. This is the front end of a typical linear power supply.

lessons.title: Zener Regulators, Clippers/Clamps, and Special Diodes
lessons.content:
We have seen diodes as one-way valves and as rectifiers. Now a quick tour of three more useful tricks, kept conceptual.

**The Zener diode: holding a steady voltage.** Normally we never push a diode hard backwards. But a special diode called a **Zener** is built to do exactly that safely. When reverse-biased past its rated **Zener voltage** (say 5.1 V), it suddenly conducts and then holds that voltage nearly constant even as current changes. Picture a relief valve that opens at a set pressure and keeps the pressure pinned there. Wire a Zener with a series resistor and you get a simple **voltage regulator / reference**: a stable voltage for the rest of the circuit. It is cheap and rugged but not super-precise, and it wastes some power in the resistor, so it is used for modest jobs rather than lab-grade references.

**Clippers: chopping off peaks.** A **clipper** (or limiter) uses a diode to cut off part of a signal that goes too high or too low. Once the signal tries to exceed the diode's threshold, the diode conducts and holds the output from going further, slicing off the peak. A common use is **protection**: clipping diodes guard a sensitive input from voltage spikes (such as static-electricity zaps).

**Clamps: shifting a signal up or down.** A **clamp** (DC restorer) does not change a signal's shape; it shifts the whole thing up or down so one edge sits at a chosen level, using a diode and a capacitor.

**A few special diodes (one line each):**
- **Schottky diode**: a faster diode with a lower drop (~0.3 V), great for high-speed and efficient power circuits.
- **Photodiode**: a diode that turns light into a small electric current; it is the 'eye' in light sensors and optical receivers.
- **Varactor**: a diode used backwards as an adjustable capacitor, where the voltage you apply tunes its capacitance, handy for tuning radios.

**Recap:** a Zener holds a steady voltage (a simple regulator), a clipper slices off peaks (often for protection), a clamp shifts a signal's level, and special diodes (Schottky, photodiode, varactor) trade speed, light-sensing, or tunable capacitance for specialized jobs.

items.prompt: What is special about a Zener diode compared with an ordinary diode?
choices: It is designed to conduct safely when reverse-biased past its Zener voltage, holding that voltage steady
It has no forward direction at all
items.explanation: A Zener is built to break down safely in reverse at a set voltage and hold that voltage nearly constant, like a pressure-relief valve. An ordinary diode is not meant to be driven hard in reverse.

items.prompt: A Zener diode plus a series resistor is commonly used as a:
items.explanation: The series resistor drops the excess voltage and the Zener pins the output at its rated voltage, giving a simple, steady reference voltage. It does not amplify or emit light.

items.prompt: A good analogy for how a Zener regulator holds its voltage is:
choices: A pressure-relief valve that opens at a set pressure and keeps the pressure pinned there
A wire that simply gets hot
items.explanation: Just as a relief valve opens at a set pressure and holds that pressure, a Zener conducts at its rated voltage and holds the voltage nearly constant. The other options do not capture the 'hold at a set level' behavior.

items.prompt: Why is a basic Zener regulator NOT ideal for very precise or high-power jobs?
choices: It cannot hold any voltage at all
It is not super-precise and wastes some power in the series resistor
It needs a transformer to function
items.explanation: A bare Zener regulator is cheap and rugged but only moderately accurate, and the series resistor always burns some power. For precision or efficiency you would use a dedicated regulator/reference IC instead.

items.prompt: What does a clipper (limiter) circuit do to a signal?
choices: Shifts its whole level up or down
Slices off the part of the signal that goes beyond a threshold
items.explanation: A clipper conducts once the signal passes a threshold and holds the output from going further, slicing off the peak. Shifting the level is a clamp's job, not a clipper's.

items.prompt: A very common practical use for clipping diodes on a circuit's input is:
choices: Protecting a sensitive input from voltage spikes (such as static zaps)
items.explanation: Clamp/clipping diodes to the supply rails conduct on overvoltage spikes and shunt them away, protecting the sensitive node. This is a standard input-protection trick, including against static electricity (ESD).

items.prompt: How does a clamp (DC restorer) differ from a clipper?
choices: A clamp slices off peaks; a clipper shifts the level
A clamp shifts the whole signal up or down without changing its shape; a clipper cuts off peaks
They are exactly the same circuit
A clamp turns AC into light
items.explanation: A clamp keeps the waveform's shape but moves the whole thing up or down so an edge sits at a chosen level; a clipper instead chops off the parts that exceed a threshold. They do opposite-sounding jobs.

items.prompt: A Schottky diode is most notable for being:
choices: Faster, with a lower forward drop (~0.3 V) than an ordinary silicon diode
items.explanation: A Schottky diode switches faster and has a lower forward drop (~0.3 V vs ~0.7 V), which makes it efficient in high-speed and power circuits. It is not a light emitter or an insulator.

items.prompt: A photodiode is a diode that:
choices: Turns light falling on it into a small electric current
items.explanation: A photodiode does the reverse of an LED: incoming light produces a small current, making it the light-sensing 'eye' in optical sensors and receivers. Emitting light is the LED's role; tuning is the varactor's.

items.prompt: A varactor diode is useful because:
choices: Its capacitance can be adjusted by the voltage applied, so it can tune circuits like radios
It produces the brightest light of any diode
It stores more energy than a battery
items.explanation: A varactor, used reverse-biased, acts as a voltage-controlled capacitor: changing the voltage changes its capacitance, which is handy for tuning radio and oscillator circuits. The other options describe different devices.

items.prompt: A simple Zener shunt voltage regulator
choices: Circuit: source, series resistor, Zener across the load
items.explanation: The series resistor drops the extra voltage and the Zener, across the load, pins the output near its rated voltage, giving a steady supply for the load.

lessons.title: What a Transistor Does: NPN/PNP, Terminals, Beta, and the Switch
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine a tiny tap that, with the lightest touch, opens or closes a fire hose. That is the magic of a **transistor**: a small control signal steers a much larger current. Because of that, a transistor can be an **amplifier** (it makes a weak signal bigger) or a **switch** (it turns something fully on or fully off). A microphone makes a tiny electrical wiggle; a transistor uses that wiggle to control a much bigger current that can drive a loudspeaker. Same idea as a small tap turning a big water valve.

**Build.** A **BJT** (bipolar junction transistor) is the most classic kind. It has **three terminals**: the **base** (B, the control tap), the **collector** (C, where the big current comes in), and the **emitter** (E, where it leaves). The rule is simple: a *small* **base current** controls a *big* **collector current**. We measure how big that multiplication is with the **current gain**, called **beta** ($\beta$):

$$I_C = \beta\, I_B$$

In words: collector current equals beta times base current. A typical beta is around 100, so 0.01 mA into the base can control 1 mA into the collector. The three currents are tied together by $I_E = I_C + I_B$ (everything that goes in at the collector and base leaves at the emitter).

BJTs come in two flavors that are mirror images: **NPN** and **PNP**. NPN is the most common; the difference is just the direction the currents and voltages run.

Used as a **switch**, the transistor sits in one of two extreme states. **Cutoff** means no base drive, so almost no collector current flows: the switch is *open* (OFF). **Saturation** means lots of base drive, so the transistor is fully turned on and the switch is *closed* (ON). In between is the **active** region, which is where amplifiers work (more on that in later lessons).

**Recap.** A transistor is a valve: a small base current controls a big collector current, with gain $\beta = I_C / I_B$. Two flavors (NPN/PNP), three terminals (base/collector/emitter), and two switch states (cutoff = off, saturation = on).

items.prompt: In the NPN symbol shown, which terminal carries the small control current that steers the large collector current?
items.explanation: The base is the control tap: a small base current steers the large collector current. The collector carries the big controlled current, and the emitter (with the arrow) carries the sum of the two.

items.prompt: What is the best one-sentence description of what a transistor does?
choices: A small control signal steers a much larger current
It stores charge like a battery
It blocks all current in both directions
It converts AC directly into DC with no other parts
items.explanation: A transistor is a control valve: a tiny control signal steers a much larger current. That single idea lets it act as an amplifier (make a signal bigger) or a switch (on/off). Storing charge is a capacitor; blocking current is closer to a reverse-biased diode.

items.prompt: Name the three terminals of a BJT.
items.explanation: A BJT has a base (the control tap), a collector (big current in), and an emitter (current out). Source/drain/gate are the terminals of a different device, the MOSFET. Anode/cathode belong to a diode.

items.prompt: A BJT has beta = 100 and a base current of 0.02 mA. What collector current does it control?
items.explanation: Use I_C = beta x I_B = 100 x 0.02 mA = 2 mA. The whole point of beta is this multiplication: a small base current controls a much larger collector current.

items.prompt: What does the current gain beta tell you?
choices: How many times bigger the collector current is than the base current
The voltage across the base-emitter junction
items.explanation: Beta = I_C / I_B is the current gain: how many times larger the controlled collector current is than the small base current. A beta of 100 means the collector current is 100 times the base current.

items.prompt: How do the three BJT currents relate to each other?
choices: I_E = I_C + I_B (emitter carries the sum)
All three currents are always equal
items.explanation: Current is conserved: everything entering at the collector and base leaves at the emitter, so I_E = I_C + I_B. Since I_B is tiny compared to I_C, the emitter and collector currents are nearly equal in practice.

items.prompt: A transistor used as a switch is fully OFF (no collector current flowing). What region is it in?
items.explanation: Cutoff means there is no base drive, so essentially no collector current flows; the switch is open (OFF). Saturation is the opposite extreme (fully ON), and active is the in-between amplifying region.

items.prompt: In the switch circuit shown, what happens when the control input goes HIGH and pushes plenty of current into the base?
choices: The transistor saturates (turns fully ON) and the load (LED/relay) is energized
The transistor goes into cutoff and the load turns off
Nothing changes; the base does not affect the load
items.explanation: Driving plenty of current into the base saturates the transistor: it acts like a closed switch, so current flows through the load and it turns on. A LOW control input gives no base drive (cutoff), opening the switch and turning the load off.

items.prompt: What is the difference between an NPN and a PNP transistor, at the beginner level?
choices: They are polarity mirror images; the currents and voltages run in opposite directions
NPN cannot be used as a switch
items.explanation: NPN and PNP are mirror images of each other: the same valve idea, but the currents and supply polarity are reversed. Both have all three terminals and both can amplify or switch. NPN is the more common starting point.

items.prompt: Why can the same device act as both an amplifier and a switch?
choices: Because a small control signal steers a large current: ride it smoothly for amplification, or slam it between off and on for switching
Because it contains two separate devices inside
Because amplifiers and switches are actually the same circuit
Because beta changes from positive to negative
items.explanation: Both uses come from one idea: the base steers the collector current. If you let the control signal vary smoothly in the active region, you get a bigger copy of it (amplifier). If you drive it hard between cutoff and saturation, you get an on/off switch.

lessons.title: Setting the Resting Point: DC Biasing, the Q-Point, and Voltage-Divider Bias
lessons.content:
**Hook.** To use a transistor as an *amplifier*, it must sit in the active region even when there is no signal yet, like an idling engine ready to rev either way. Setting that resting state is called **biasing**, and the resting point is the **Q-point** (quiescent operating point). Get it wrong and your amplified signal gets chopped off (clipped) at the top or bottom.

**Build.** The Q-point is just the steady (DC, no-signal) collector current $I_{CQ}$ and collector-emitter voltage $V_{CEQ}$ that the transistor sits at. We want it roughly in the *middle*, so the signal has room to swing up and down without hitting a limit.

The trouble is **beta**. Beta varies a lot between individual transistors (easily 3 to 1) and drifts with temperature. A naive design that sets the base current directly lets $I_C = \beta I_B$ wander all over the place. A good bias design makes the Q-point depend on *resistors*, not on the unreliable beta.

The standard, dependable solution is **voltage-divider bias**: two resistors ($R_1$ and $R_2$) form a divider that fixes a steady base voltage $V_B$, and an **emitter resistor** $R_E$ sets the current. The emitter resistor self-corrects: if current tries to rise, the emitter voltage rises, which reduces the base-emitter drive and pushes the current back down (a stabilizing feedback). With a stiff divider,

$$V_B \approx V_{CC}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad I_C \approx \frac{V_B - 0.7\,\text{V}}{R_E}.$$

Notice beta does not appear, so the Q-point is stable.

A helpful picture is the **load line**: a straight line on the graph of collector current versus collector-emitter voltage, fixed by the supply and the resistors. The Q-point sits on that line. Put it near the middle for the most symmetric swing.

**Recap.** Biasing parks the transistor at a stable resting Q-point in the middle of the active region. Voltage-divider bias plus an emitter resistor sets that point with resistors instead of the unreliable beta, and the load line shows where the Q-point lives.

items.prompt: In the voltage-divider-biased amplifier shown, what is the job of the two divider resistors R1 and R2?
choices: They set a steady base voltage that fixes the resting operating point
They amplify the input signal directly
They block all DC from the transistor
They are only there to waste power
items.explanation: R1 and R2 form a voltage divider that holds the base at a steady DC voltage. Together with the emitter resistor, this fixes a stable Q-point that does not depend on the transistor's beta.

items.prompt: What is the Q-point (quiescent operating point) of an amplifier?
choices: The steady DC current and voltage the transistor sits at with no signal applied
The maximum current the transistor can ever handle
The point where the transistor melts
The frequency at which gain is highest
items.explanation: The Q-point is the resting (quiescent) DC operating point: the collector current and collector-emitter voltage with no signal. The signal then rides up and down around this point, so it should be centered for the most room to swing.

items.prompt: Why do we want the Q-point near the middle of the load line?
choices: So the signal has equal room to swing up and down without clipping
Because the transistor only works at the exact center
To minimize the supply voltage needed
To make beta as large as possible
items.explanation: A centered Q-point leaves equal headroom in both directions, so the amplified signal can swing up and down symmetrically before hitting a limit (clipping). If the Q-point sits near either extreme, that half of the signal clips first.

items.prompt: Why is beta a troublemaker for biasing?
choices: It varies widely between transistors and drifts with temperature, so a Q-point that depends on it is unstable
It makes the transistor switch too fast
It only matters at high frequency
items.explanation: Beta can differ by 3 to 1 between supposedly identical parts and changes with temperature. A bias scheme that relies on beta gives an unpredictable Q-point, so good designs make the operating point depend on resistors instead.

items.prompt: Why is voltage-divider bias preferred over a single base resistor (fixed bias)?
choices: It sets the Q-point with resistor ratios, making it nearly independent of beta
It makes beta irrelevant to the transistor's physics
It removes the need for a power supply
items.explanation: Voltage-divider bias fixes the base voltage and lets the emitter resistor set the current, so the Q-point depends on resistor values rather than beta. Fixed bias sets the base current directly, letting I_C = beta x I_B drift with the unreliable beta.

items.prompt: How does the emitter resistor R_E help stabilize the operating point?
choices: If current rises, the emitter voltage rises, reducing the base-emitter drive and pushing current back down
It has no effect on stability
items.explanation: The emitter resistor provides self-correcting (negative) feedback. A rise in current lifts the emitter voltage, which shrinks the base-emitter voltage that drives the transistor, counteracting the original rise. This keeps the Q-point steady.

items.prompt: Voltage-divider bias: supply = 12 V, R1 = 47k, R2 = 10k, R_E = 1k, and base-emitter drop ~0.7 V. Roughly what is the resting collector current?
items.explanation: First the base voltage: V_B = 12 x 10k/(47k+10k) = about 2.1 V. Then I_C is about (V_B - 0.7)/R_E = (2.1 - 0.7)/1000 = 1.4 mA. Notice beta never entered the calculation, which is the whole point.

items.prompt: On the load-line graph shown, where does the Q-point sit?
choices: On the load line, at the chosen resting current and voltage
At the highest possible voltage on the axis
items.explanation: The load line is the set of all possible (current, voltage) operating points allowed by the supply and resistors. The Q-point is one specific spot on that line, chosen by the bias network, ideally near the middle for the most symmetric swing.

items.prompt: What does the load line represent?
choices: The straight line of allowed collector current versus collector-emitter voltage, set by the supply and resistors
The gain of the amplifier versus frequency
The temperature of the transistor over time
The beta of the transistor versus current
items.explanation: The load line plots collector current against collector-emitter voltage for the circuit's supply and resistors. The transistor must operate somewhere on that line; the bias network picks the Q-point along it.

lessons.title: How an Amplifier Sees a Small Signal
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Once a transistor is biased at a steady resting Q-point, a small input signal rides on top of that resting state like ripples on a calm pond. To understand amplification, we only need to know how the transistor responds to those *small* wiggles, not the whole picture at once. This is called **small-signal** thinking.

**Build.** The key idea is **transconductance**, written $g_m$. It answers: for a small change in the base-emitter voltage, how much does the collector current change? A bigger $g_m$ means a small input voltage wiggle produces a bigger output current wiggle, which is the raw material of gain. The handy fact is that $g_m$ grows with the bias current:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T},$$

where $V_T$ is the **thermal voltage**, about 25 mV (millivolts) at room temperature. So at a resting current of 1 mA, $g_m$ is about 40 mA per volt. The lesson: run more current, get more gain capability (within limits).

A second useful idea is the **input resistance** the signal sees looking into the base, often called $r_\pi$. It tells you how much the input source is loaded. Together, $g_m$ and the input resistance let us predict an amplifier's voltage gain and how it interacts with whatever drives it and whatever it drives.

You do not need heavy algebra to keep the intuition: **transconductance turns a small input voltage into a proportional output current**, and the bigger that conversion, the more gain the stage can deliver. Real amplifiers then trade some of that raw gain for stability and lower distortion (for example by adding an emitter resistor that is not bypassed).

**Recap.** Small-signal analysis treats the input as gentle ripples on the resting Q-point. The star quantity is transconductance $g_m = I_C / V_T$: it converts a small input voltage into an output current and sets how much gain the transistor can provide.

items.prompt: What does 'small-signal' analysis assume?
choices: The signal is a small wiggle riding on top of the steady resting Q-point
The signal is so large it drives the transistor between cutoff and saturation
There is no bias current at all
items.explanation: Small-signal analysis treats the input as small ripples on top of the DC resting point, so the transistor's response can be treated as roughly linear. This is exactly the regime a good amplifier operates in, well away from clipping.

items.prompt: In plain words, what does transconductance (g_m) describe?
choices: How much the collector current changes for a small change in base-emitter voltage
How much voltage the transistor can withstand
How fast the transistor heats up
The ratio of collector to base current
items.explanation: Transconductance is the conversion from a small input voltage wiggle to an output current wiggle: g_m = change in I_C / change in V_BE. The collector-to-base current ratio is beta, a different quantity.

items.prompt: How does transconductance depend on the bias current?
choices: It grows with the bias current: g_m = I_C / V_T
It does not depend on current at all
items.explanation: g_m = I_C / V_T, so transconductance is directly proportional to the resting collector current. Run more current and the transistor converts a given input voltage into a larger output current, raising the available gain.

items.prompt: The thermal voltage V_T at room temperature is about:
items.explanation: V_T is about 25 mV near room temperature. (The 0.7 V figure is the typical base-emitter turn-on drop, a different number; don't confuse the two.)

items.prompt: A transistor is biased at I_C = 1 mA. Using V_T = 25 mV, roughly what is g_m?
items.explanation: g_m = I_C / V_T = 1 mA / 25 mV = 0.04 A/V = 40 mA per volt. This is the most-used quick number in transistor amplifier design.

items.prompt: Why does a higher bias current give a transistor more gain capability?
choices: Higher current raises g_m, so a given input voltage produces a larger output current
Higher current lowers g_m, reducing distortion
Current has nothing to do with gain
It reduces the thermal voltage to zero
items.explanation: Since g_m = I_C / V_T, more current means more transconductance, so the same small input voltage drives a bigger output current. There are practical limits (power, distortion), but the basic trend is more current, more potential gain.

items.prompt: What does the small-signal input resistance looking into the base tell a designer?
choices: How heavily the amplifier loads the source that drives it
items.explanation: The input resistance at the base sets how much current the amplifier draws from whatever drives it, i.e. how much it loads the source. A higher input resistance loads the source less, which is often desirable.

items.prompt: Real amplifiers often add an unbypassed emitter resistor that reduces gain. Why accept less gain?
choices: It trades raw gain for better stability, lower distortion, and a more predictable gain
It eliminates the need for biasing
It makes the transistor switch faster
items.explanation: An unbypassed emitter resistor adds local feedback. You give up some of the raw gain in exchange for a gain that depends mostly on resistor values (so it is predictable and stable) and is more linear (less distortion). It is a common and worthwhile trade.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the small-signal view of amplification?
choices: Transconductance turns a small input voltage into a proportional output current, which becomes a bigger output voltage across the load
The transistor stores the input and releases it later
Amplification requires the transistor to be in cutoff
Gain comes only from beta and nothing else
items.explanation: The chain is: small input voltage -> (via g_m) -> output current wiggle -> (across the load resistor) -> bigger output voltage. That voltage gain is what makes the stage an amplifier; beta alone is the current relationship, not the whole story.

lessons.title: The Three Amplifier Shapes and the Differential Pair
lessons.content:
**Hook.** There is more than one way to wire a transistor into an amplifier, and each shape is good at a different job, like choosing the right tool from a small toolbox. There are three basic single-transistor configurations, named by which terminal is shared (common) between the input and the output.

**Build.** **Common-emitter (CE)** is the general-purpose gain stage. Input goes to the base, output comes from the collector. It gives a large voltage gain, but it is **inverting**: when the input goes up, the output goes down. Reach for this when you simply need to make a signal bigger.

**Common-collector**, better known as the **emitter follower**, is a **buffer**. Its voltage gain is only about 1 (it does not make the signal bigger), but it has a high input resistance and a low output resistance. That means it can listen to a delicate source without disturbing it and then drive a heavy load. Use it to connect a weak source to a demanding load, like an impedance translator.

**Common-base (CB)** sends the input into the emitter and takes the output from the collector. It is non-inverting and shines at very high (radio) frequencies because of how it handles internal capacitance.

Finally, the **differential pair** is two matched transistors sharing a common tail current source. It amplifies the *difference* between its two inputs while ignoring (rejecting) any signal that is common to both. That ability to grab a tiny difference and throw away shared noise is so valuable that the differential pair is the **input stage of nearly every op-amp** (operational amplifier) and comparator. It is the bridge from single transistors to the integrated building blocks that follow.

**Recap.** Common-emitter = big inverting voltage gain; common-collector/emitter-follower = unity-gain buffer; common-base = wideband non-inverting. The differential pair amplifies the difference of two inputs and rejects the common part, which is why it sits at the heart of op-amps.

items.prompt: How are the three single-transistor amplifier configurations named?
choices: By which terminal is common (shared) between the input and the output
By the color of the transistor
By how much current they draw
By the brand of transistor used
items.explanation: The names come from the terminal shared between input and output: common-emitter, common-collector, and common-base. That shared terminal is what gives each configuration its characteristic behavior.

items.prompt: Which configuration is the general-purpose gain stage with a large but inverting voltage gain?
items.explanation: The common-emitter stage gives a large voltage gain and inverts the signal (input up, output down). The emitter follower has gain near 1, and the common-base is non-inverting; neither is the standard high-gain inverting stage.

items.prompt: What does 'inverting' mean for the common-emitter amplifier?
choices: When the input voltage goes up, the output voltage goes down
It reduces the signal to zero
It reverses the direction of current gain
items.explanation: Inverting means the output moves in the opposite direction to the input: a rising input produces a falling output (a 180-degree flip). It still amplifies; the waveform is just upside down.

items.prompt: You need to connect a delicate, high-resistance sensor to a heavy load without disturbing the sensor, at roughly unity voltage gain. Which configuration?
items.explanation: The emitter follower is a buffer: high input resistance (so it barely loads the sensor), low output resistance (so it can drive a heavy load), and a voltage gain of about 1. That is exactly the impedance-matching/buffering job described.

items.prompt: The emitter follower has a voltage gain of only about 1. Why is it still useful?
choices: It has high input resistance and low output resistance, so it buffers a weak source to drive a heavy load
It secretly has the highest voltage gain of all
It is the only one that inverts the signal
items.explanation: Even without voltage gain, the follower provides current gain and impedance translation: it presents a light load to the source and a strong drive to the load. That isolation is valuable whenever a source cannot directly drive a load.

items.prompt: Which configuration is non-inverting and especially good at very high (radio) frequencies?
choices: All three are equally good at high frequency
items.explanation: The common-base stage is non-inverting and handles internal capacitance in a way that gives excellent high-frequency performance, which is why it shows up in radio-frequency circuits. The common-emitter, by contrast, is more bandwidth-limited.

items.prompt: In the differential pair shown, what does the circuit amplify?
choices: The difference between the two inputs (v1 minus v2)
Only the larger of the two inputs
The sum of the two inputs
Whichever input is connected to ground
items.explanation: A differential pair amplifies the difference between its two inputs while rejecting any signal common to both. Grabbing a small difference and throwing away shared noise is precisely what makes it so useful.

items.prompt: Why is the differential pair the foundation of nearly every op-amp and comparator?
choices: It amplifies the difference of two inputs while rejecting shared (common-mode) signals, which is exactly what an op-amp input needs
It is the cheapest possible circuit
It can only be built from one transistor
It eliminates the need for any gain stages
items.explanation: An op-amp's defining feature is a high-gain difference amplifier with two matched inputs that ignores common-mode signals. The differential pair provides exactly that, so it serves as the op-amp's input stage.

items.prompt: What does it mean that a differential pair 'rejects the common-mode' signal?
choices: A signal applied equally to both inputs produces little or no output; only the difference is amplified
It rejects all signals and outputs nothing
It amplifies noise more than signal
It only works with DC inputs
items.explanation: Common-mode means the same signal on both inputs (often noise picked up by both wires). The pair largely ignores that and amplifies only the difference, so shared interference is rejected while the wanted difference signal gets through.

items.prompt: Match the job to the configuration: 'I just need to make this signal much bigger.'
choices: None of the configurations can amplify
items.explanation: For straightforward voltage gain, the common-emitter stage is the go-to choice (accepting that it inverts). The emitter follower has gain near 1, so it would not make the signal bigger.

lessons.title: What a MOSFET Is: A Voltage-Controlled Valve
lessons.content:
Inside the chip running your phone are billions of tiny switches. Almost all of them are **MOSFETs**. So what is one?

A MOSFET is a kind of **transistor** — think of a transistor as an electrically controlled *valve* for electric current. Just as a faucet lets a small turn of the handle control a big flow of water, a transistor lets a small control signal steer a much larger current.

You already met one kind of valve, the **BJT** (bipolar junction transistor). The BJT's handle is a small *current*. A MOSFET's handle is different: it is a **voltage** on a terminal called the **gate**. MOSFET stands for *Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor* — the long name just says the gate is a metal plate sitting on a thin layer of insulating glass (oxide) over the silicon. Put a voltage on that plate and its electric field reaches through the glass to open a path for current underneath. Take the voltage away and the path closes.

Because the gate sits on an *insulator*, almost **no current flows into the gate** — it behaves like a touch-sensitive plate, not a wire you push current through. That gives a MOSFET an enormously high **input resistance** (the gate barely loads whatever drives it).

MOSFETs are the workhorse of all digital chips and of power switching (motor drivers, dimmers, power supplies).

**Recap:** a MOSFET is a transistor — a valve for current — but it is controlled by a *voltage* on an insulated gate, and almost no current flows into that gate.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what does a transistor (such as a MOSFET) do?
choices: It stores electric charge like a battery for later use
It lets a small control signal steer a much larger current, like a valve
It converts AC voltage into DC voltage
It blocks all current in both directions
items.explanation: A transistor is an electrically controlled valve: a small signal at the control terminal governs a larger current through the device. It is not a charge store (that is a capacitor/battery) nor a rectifier (that is a diode).

items.prompt: What is the MOSFET's control terminal called, and what controls the device through it?
choices: The base, controlled by a current
The gate, controlled by a voltage
The drain, controlled by a magnetic field
items.explanation: A MOSFET is controlled by a voltage on its gate. (The base, controlled by a current, is the BJT's control terminal — a tempting mix-up since both are transistors.)

items.prompt: What does the 'oxide' in Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor refer to?
choices: A rusted metal contact that must be cleaned off
A thin insulating layer that separates the gate from the silicon
A coating that conducts current into the gate
The semiconductor material the channel is made of
items.explanation: The oxide is a very thin layer of insulating glass between the metal gate and the semiconductor. Because it insulates, the gate's voltage acts through its electric field rather than by injecting current.

items.prompt: Why is almost no current able to flow into a MOSFET's gate?
choices: The gate sits on an insulating oxide layer, so it is not a conductive path
The gate is made of a special non-metal
Current only flows into the gate at high frequency
items.explanation: The insulating oxide under the gate blocks a DC conduction path, so the gate behaves like a touch-sensitive plate. This is why the gate draws essentially no steady current.

items.prompt: Because almost no current flows into the gate, a MOSFET has a very high:
choices: Input resistance (it barely loads whatever drives it)
items.explanation: Drawing almost no input current means the gate hardly loads the source driving it — that is the definition of a very high input resistance, a hallmark MOSFET advantage.

items.prompt: Which everyday claim about MOSFETs is correct?
choices: They are rarely used and mostly historical
There are billions of MOSFET switches inside a single phone chip
They only work at very high radio frequencies
They can only amplify, never switch
items.explanation: MOSFETs are the workhorse of digital chips; a modern phone processor contains billions of them. They are used everywhere and can both switch and amplify.

items.prompt: The water-faucet analogy maps onto a MOSFET how?
choices: The handle is the gate voltage; the water flow is the current through the device
The water is the gate voltage; the handle is the output
There is no flow control, only on or off
items.explanation: A small turn of the handle (gate voltage) controls a large water flow (current through the device, from drain to source). This captures the idea of a small control governing a large flow.

items.prompt: The key difference between how you control a BJT versus a MOSFET is:
choices: A BJT uses light; a MOSFET uses sound
A BJT is controlled by a current into its base; a MOSFET by a voltage on its gate
A BJT is controlled by a voltage; a MOSFET by a current
There is no difference; the terminals are just renamed
items.explanation: Both are transistors (valves), but the BJT's handle is a base current while the MOSFET's handle is a gate voltage. That single difference drives most of the behavior differences between them.

items.prompt: Name two places MOSFETs do the heavy lifting in everyday electronics.
choices: Digital logic chips and power switching (motor drivers, dimmers)
items.explanation: MOSFETs are the switches inside every digital chip and the workhorses of power switching such as motor drivers, light dimmers, and switching power supplies.

items.prompt: Why is the analogy 'the gate is a touch-sensitive plate' helpful?
choices: Because the gate physically moves when touched
Because the gate responds to a voltage (its field) without needing current to flow into it
Because the gate must be touched to work
items.explanation: Like a touch plate that senses presence without drawing meaningful current, the gate responds to the voltage applied to it (through its electric field) rather than to a current pushed into it.

items.prompt: A MOSFET symbol with gate, drain, and source. Which terminal is the control input?
items.explanation: The gate is the control terminal; current flows between drain and source, and the gate voltage decides how much. The image shows the gate lead separated from the channel by the insulating oxide gap.

lessons.title: Gate, Drain, Source, and the Threshold Voltage
lessons.content:
Now let's name the parts and learn the one number that decides whether a MOSFET is on or off.

A MOSFET has three main terminals (a fourth, the *body*, is usually tied to the source and ignored for now):

- **Gate (G):** the control plate. Put a voltage here.
- **Drain (D)** and **Source (S):** the two ends of the current path, called the **channel**. Current flows between them when the channel is open.

Think of drain and source as the two ends of a pipe and the gate as the handle that opens or closes it.

The deciding number is the **threshold voltage**, written $V_{th}$ (sometimes $V_T$ or $V_{GS(th)}$). It is the gate-to-source voltage needed before the channel opens enough to conduct. We write the gate-to-source voltage as $V_{GS}$ (the voltage *of the gate relative to the source*).

For the most common type (an *enhancement* N-channel MOSFET):

- If $V_{GS}$ is **below** $V_{th}$, the channel is closed — the device is **off**, almost no current flows.
- If $V_{GS}$ rises **above** $V_{th}$, the channel opens — the device turns **on**, and the higher you push $V_{GS}$, the wider the channel and the more current it allows.

For example, a small logic MOSFET might have $V_{th} \approx 1\ \text{V}$: hold the gate at 0 V and it is off; raise it to 3 V and it is comfortably on.

**Recap:** current flows from drain to source through the channel; the gate voltage controls it; and the channel only opens once $V_{GS}$ exceeds the threshold $V_{th}$.

items.prompt: Which three terminals does a MOSFET have (ignoring the body)?
items.explanation: A MOSFET's terminals are the gate (control), and the drain and source (the two ends of the current path). Base/collector/emitter are the BJT's terminals.

items.prompt: Current through a MOSFET normally flows between which two terminals?
items.explanation: The controlled current flows through the channel between drain and source. The gate only sets how open that channel is; it does not carry the main current.

items.prompt: What does the threshold voltage V_th tell you?
choices: The maximum current the device can carry
The gate-to-source voltage needed before the channel starts to conduct
The voltage at which the device is destroyed
The voltage across the drain and source when off
items.explanation: V_th is the gate-to-source voltage that must be exceeded for the channel to open and conduct. Below it the device is essentially off; above it the device turns on.

items.prompt: What does the symbol V_GS stand for?
choices: The voltage of the gate relative to the source
The current from gate to source
The voltage across the drain resistor
items.explanation: V_GS is the gate-to-source voltage — the gate's potential measured relative to the source. It is the quantity compared against V_th to decide on/off.

items.prompt: An enhancement N-channel MOSFET has V_th = 1 V. With V_GS = 0 V, the device is:
items.explanation: With V_GS (0 V) below V_th (1 V), the channel has not opened, so the device is off and conducts almost no current. You must raise V_GS above 1 V to turn it on.

items.prompt: For the same device (V_th = 1 V), what happens as you raise V_GS from 1 V toward 3 V?
choices: Nothing changes; it is already maxed out
The channel widens and the device allows more current
The threshold voltage rises with it
items.explanation: Once V_GS is above V_th, pushing it higher widens the channel and lets more current flow. The amount V_GS exceeds threshold sets how hard the device is turned on.

items.prompt: A good mechanical analogy for drain, source, and gate is:
choices: Drain and source are the two ends of a pipe; the gate is the handle that opens it
The gate is a pipe; drain and source are handles
The gate is the water and the drain is the pipe
items.explanation: Drain and source are the two ends of the current pipe (channel); the gate is the handle (voltage) that opens or closes it. This matches how current flows D-to-S while the gate controls it.

items.prompt: The body (fourth terminal) of a MOSFET is, in most beginner circuits:
choices: Tied to the source and largely ignored
Used as the main control input
items.explanation: In simple use the body is tied to the source, so the device acts as a three-terminal part. (It matters in more advanced stacked circuits, but not for a first pass.)

items.prompt: If a datasheet lists V_GS(th) = 2 V, the cleanest way to keep this N-channel device firmly OFF is to:
choices: Hold V_GS at 0 V (gate at the source potential)
Leave the gate floating and hope
Apply exactly 2 V to the gate
items.explanation: Holding V_GS at 0 V keeps it well below the 2 V threshold, so the channel stays closed. Floating gates can drift up and accidentally turn on; 5 V would turn it on; 2 V is right at the edge.

items.prompt: Why is V_GS measured relative to the source rather than to ground?
choices: Because the source is always at ground
Because what opens the channel is the gate's voltage compared to the channel's source end
It does not matter which reference you use
items.explanation: The channel opens based on the field between gate and the source end of the channel, so the relevant control quantity is gate-relative-to-source. If the source is not at ground, using ground as the reference would give the wrong answer.

lessons.title: The MOSFET as a Switch and the Basis of Digital Logic
lessons.content:
The simplest and most important job a MOSFET does is act as an electronic **on/off switch** — no moving parts, controlled by a voltage. This is the foundation of all digital logic.

**The two extremes.** When you drive the gate *hard* (push $V_{GS}$ well above $V_{th}$), the channel opens wide and the device acts like a small **resistor** between drain and source. We call that resistance the **on-resistance**, $R_{DS(on)}$. A good switching MOSFET has a tiny $R_{DS(on)}$ (milliohms), so almost no voltage is wasted across it when on. When the gate is at 0 V (below $V_{th}$), the channel is closed and the device is an open switch — essentially no current.

**A real switch circuit.** A common arrangement is the **low-side switch**: the load (a lamp or motor) connects to the supply on top, the MOSFET sits below it, and the source goes to ground. A high gate signal turns the MOSFET on and current flows through the load; a low gate signal turns it off. A tiny microcontroller pin, drawing almost no current, can switch a big load this way — exactly because the gate needs voltage, not current.

**Digital logic.** Computers represent 1 and 0 as 'high voltage' and 'low voltage.' MOSFET switches route those voltages. The key trick is **CMOS** (Complementary MOS): pair an N-channel device with a P-channel device so that in any steady state, one is off. That breaks the path from supply to ground, so a CMOS gate uses almost **no power while just holding a value** — it only burns energy when it switches. This near-zero static power is why CMOS scaled to billions of gates on one chip.

**Recap:** drive the gate hard and a MOSFET is a tiny resistor (on); drop the gate and it is an open switch (off). Pairing N and P devices (CMOS) gives near-zero standby power — the basis of all modern digital chips.

items.prompt: When a MOSFET is driven hard ON, it behaves most like:
choices: A small resistor between drain and source
items.explanation: Driven hard on, the channel is wide open and the device looks like a small resistor, R_DS(on), between drain and source. An open circuit is the OFF state, not the ON state.

items.prompt: What is R_DS(on)?
choices: The resistance of the gate insulator
The drain-to-source resistance of the device when fully turned on
The threshold voltage of the device
items.explanation: R_DS(on) is the small resistance seen from drain to source when the MOSFET is fully on. A low R_DS(on) wastes little voltage/power in the on state.

items.prompt: Why is a low R_DS(on) desirable in a switching MOSFET?
choices: It makes the device switch slower
It wastes little power and little voltage when the switch is on
items.explanation: Less on-resistance means less voltage dropped and less power (heat) wasted while conducting, so more of the supply reaches the load. It does not change V_th or gate current.

items.prompt: In a low-side switch, where does the MOSFET sit relative to the load?
choices: Above the load, between supply and load
Below the load, between the load and ground
items.explanation: A low-side switch places the MOSFET between the load and ground (the 'low' side). The load connects to the supply on top; the MOSFET's source goes to ground.

items.prompt: Why can a tiny microcontroller pin switch a large motor through a MOSFET?
choices: The pin supplies all the motor current directly
The gate needs only a voltage, not meaningful current, so the small pin can drive it
The MOSFET amplifies the pin's heat
items.explanation: Because the gate is voltage-controlled and draws almost no steady current, a weak pin can set the gate voltage while the MOSFET handles the heavy motor current between drain and source.

items.prompt: In digital logic, a '1' and a '0' are physically represented by:
choices: A high voltage and a low voltage
Two different currents into the gate
items.explanation: Digital circuits encode 1 and 0 as high and low voltage levels. MOSFET switches route these voltages, which is why voltage-controlled devices are a natural fit for logic.

items.prompt: What does CMOS stand for, and what is its defining idea?
choices: Compact MOS; making transistors as small as possible
Complementary MOS; pairing an N-channel and a P-channel device so one is always off in a steady state
Continuous MOS; running both devices on at once
Charged MOS; storing data as charge
items.explanation: CMOS = Complementary MOS. It pairs an N-channel and a P-channel transistor so that in any held state one of them is off, breaking the supply-to-ground path.

items.prompt: Why does a static CMOS logic gate consume almost no power while just holding a value?
choices: Because one of the complementary transistors is always off, so no path runs from supply to ground
Because it disconnects from the supply entirely
Because it stores power for later
items.explanation: In a held state one complementary device is off, breaking the direct supply-to-ground current path, so steady (static) current is near zero. Power is spent mainly during switching transitions.

items.prompt: A MOSFET switch carries 10 A with R_DS(on) = 20 milliohms. The power wasted as heat in the switch is:
items.explanation: Conduction loss is I squared times R: (10 A) squared times 0.020 ohm = 100 times 0.020 = 2 W. A lower R_DS(on) would waste less.

items.prompt: The near-zero static power of CMOS is important because it:
choices: Lets only a few transistors fit on a chip
Allowed billions of logic gates to be packed onto one chip without melting it
Makes the chip switch more slowly
Removes the need for any power supply
items.explanation: If every idle gate burned power, billions of them would generate impossible heat. Near-zero static power is exactly what let CMOS scale to billions of gates per chip.

items.prompt: In the low-side switch shown, what makes current flow through the load?
choices: Driving the gate signal high so the MOSFET turns on
items.explanation: A high gate signal raises V_GS above threshold, turning the MOSFET on so current flows from supply, through the load, through the device to ground. A low gate keeps it off.

lessons.title: N-channel vs P-channel, and Triode vs Saturation
lessons.content:
So far we used the most common MOSFET. Now meet its mirror image and learn the two 'moods' a conducting MOSFET can be in.

**N-channel vs P-channel.** There are two flavors:

- An **N-channel** MOSFET turns on when the gate is made **positive** relative to the source (a positive $V_{GS}$ above $V_{th}$). It is the one we have been using.
- A **P-channel** MOSFET is the mirror image: it turns on when the gate is made **negative** relative to the source. It is often used as a 'high-side' switch (above the load) and is the partner device in CMOS.

Think of N-channel as 'push the gate up to turn on' and P-channel as 'pull the gate down to turn on.'

**Enhancement vs depletion.** Almost every MOSFET you meet is *enhancement-mode*: off at $V_{GS}=0$, you must build up (enhance) the channel by raising $V_{GS}$. A rarer *depletion-mode* device already conducts at $V_{GS}=0$ and you turn it off by pushing the gate the other way. For beginners: assume enhancement unless told otherwise.

**Triode vs saturation regions.** When a MOSFET is on, the voltage across it (drain-to-source, $V_{DS}$) decides its behavior:

- For **small** $V_{DS}$, it acts like a **resistor** — current rises as you raise $V_{DS}$. This is the **triode** (or *linear*) region, and it is the switch-on state from the last lesson.
- Once $V_{DS}$ gets large enough, the current **levels off** and stays roughly constant no matter how much more $V_{DS}$ you add. This is the **saturation** region, and it is the state used for *amplifying* (next lesson), because the device then behaves like a controlled current source.

**Recap:** N-channel turns on with a positive gate, P-channel with a negative gate. A conducting MOSFET acts like a resistor at low $V_{DS}$ (triode — good for switching) and like a constant-current source at high $V_{DS}$ (saturation — good for amplifying).

items.prompt: An N-channel enhancement MOSFET turns on when the gate is made:
choices: Positive relative to the source (above V_th)
items.explanation: N-channel devices turn on with a positive gate-to-source voltage above threshold. (P-channel is the opposite — it turns on with a negative gate.)

items.prompt: A P-channel MOSFET is the mirror image: it turns on when the gate is made:
items.explanation: A P-channel device turns on when its gate is pulled negative relative to the source — the mirror of the N-channel. 'Push up for N, pull down for P.'

items.prompt: Which device is commonly used as a high-side switch and as the partner in CMOS?
choices: Neither; CMOS uses only one type
items.explanation: P-channel devices are convenient on the high side (above the load) and serve as the complementary partner to the N-channel device in CMOS logic.

items.prompt: An enhancement-mode MOSFET at V_GS = 0 is:
choices: On, and you turn it off by raising V_GS
Off, and you must raise V_GS to build (enhance) a channel
Always on regardless of gate voltage
items.explanation: Enhancement-mode means there is no channel at V_GS = 0; you enhance one by raising V_GS past threshold. This is the default behavior for nearly all common MOSFETs.

items.prompt: How does a depletion-mode MOSFET differ from an enhancement-mode one?
choices: It conducts already at V_GS = 0 and is turned off by pushing the gate the other way
It is controlled by current instead of voltage
items.explanation: A depletion device has a channel present at V_GS = 0, so it conducts by default and you deplete (turn off) the channel by driving the gate the opposite direction. It is far less common than enhancement.

items.prompt: For small V_DS, a conducting MOSFET is in the triode region and acts like:
choices: A resistor whose current rises with V_DS
items.explanation: In the triode (linear) region the device behaves like a resistor: current grows roughly in proportion to V_DS. This is the state used when the MOSFET is a closed switch.

items.prompt: In the saturation region (larger V_DS, device on), the drain current:
choices: Levels off and stays roughly constant as V_DS increases
items.explanation: In saturation the current flattens out and stays nearly constant despite increasing V_DS — the device acts like a controlled current source. This is the region used for amplification.

items.prompt: Which region is used when a MOSFET acts as an ON switch, and which when it amplifies?
choices: Saturation for switch-on; triode for amplifying
Triode (resistor-like) for switch-on; saturation (current-source-like) for amplifying
Both jobs use the off region
items.explanation: A closed switch sits deep in triode (small voltage drop, resistor-like). An amplifier biases the device in saturation, where it behaves like a controlled current source. (Note: confusingly, 'saturation' means the opposite state from a saturated BJT.)

items.prompt: An easy memory hook for the two channel types is:
choices: Push the gate up to turn on an N-channel; pull it down to turn on a P-channel
Pull down for N; push up for P
Both need zero gate voltage to turn on
items.explanation: N-channel: positive (up) gate turns it on. P-channel: negative (down) gate turns it on. 'Up for N, down for P' captures the mirror-image control.

items.prompt: In the output-characteristics graph (I_D vs V_DS), the flat right-hand part of each curve represents:
choices: The saturation region, where current is roughly constant
items.explanation: Each curve rises like a resistor near the origin (triode) then flattens out (saturation), where current stays nearly constant as V_DS grows. Higher gate voltage gives a higher flat current.

items.prompt: If you are not told the mode of a MOSFET in a beginner problem, the safe assumption is:
items.explanation: The overwhelming majority of MOSFETs are enhancement-mode (off at V_GS = 0), so that is the default assumption unless the problem says depletion.

lessons.title: Biasing and the Common-Source Amplifier
lessons.content:
